stem: Which nerve will get damaged due to the fracture shown in the below image?
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• The given image shows fracture of the medial epicondyle of the humerus.
• The ulnar nerve runs posterior to the medial epicondyle of humerus.
• Hence, a fracture of the medial epicondyle of the humerus will cause damage to the ulnar nerve.
• The nerve roots of the ulnar nerve are C7, C8 and T1.

Other Options:
Option A: The median nerve is not damaged in fractures of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. The median nerve runs in the cubital fossa anterior to the intercondylar region.
Option B: The radial nerve is not damaged in fractures of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. It is damaged in fractures of the mid-shaft of the humerus.
Option D: The median nerve is not damaged in fractures of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Hence, both A and C are not the correct answers.

stem: Superior gluteal nerve supplies all of the following, except
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Piriformis is supplied by the nerve to piriformis originating from the sacral plexus.
• The superior gluteal nerve supplies:
1. Gluteus medius
2. Gluteus minimus
3. Tensor fascia lata
• The inferior gluteal nerve supplies the gluteus maximus.
• Paralysis of the superior gluteal nerve leads to Trendelenburg gait during walking.

Other Options:
Option B: Gluteus medius is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. It originates from the gluteal surface of the ilium and inserts on the greater trochanter of the femur.
Option C: Gluteus minimus is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. It originates from the gluteal surface of the ilium and inserts on the greater trochanter of the femur.
Option D: Tensor fascia lata is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. It originates from the anterior superior iliac spine and anterior aspect of the iliac crest. It descends and is attached to the deep fascia and the superficial fascia of the iliotibial band.

stem: All of the following are in anterior relationship with 3rd part of duodenum, except
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The duodenum has 4 parts: D1 of 5 cm, D2 of 7.5 cm, D3 of 10 cm, D4 of 2.5 cm.
• D3 is the horizontal part of the duodenum. It lies at the vertebral level of L3.
• Structures lying anteriorly to D3 are:
1. Root of mesentery
2. Superior mesenteric vessels
3. Small bowel loops
• Structures lying posterior to D3 are:
1. Psoas major muscle
2. Right ureter
3. Inferior vena cava
4. Aorta
• The gall bladder does not lie in relation to D3. The neck of the gallbladder is related to D1. The fundus of the gallbladder is related to the transverse colon.

Other Options:
Option B: The root of mesentery lies anteriorly to the third part of the duodenum. It crosses anteriorly from the left side to the right side. It extends from duodeno-jejunal junction to the right sacro-iliac joint.
Option C: The superior mesenteric vessels lie anteriorly to the third part of the duodenum. Obstruction of D3 between superior mesenteric vessels and aorta leads to superior mesenteric syndrome or Wilkie’s syndrome.
Option D: The jejunal coils lie anteriorly to the third part of the duodenum. The duodenum is the first part of the small intestine, whereas the jejunum is the second part of the small intestine.

stem: Correct statement regarding the muscle highlighted in the below given image
options: It causes retraction of the scapula
Elevation of scapula is brought about by it
Rotation of the scapula depresses the glenoid cavity
It rotates the scapula tilting the glenoid cavity superiorly
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The medial border on the dorsal aspect of scapula has 3 muscle insertions: Levator scapulae, Rhomboid minor, and Rhomboid major.
• The muscle marked in the image is rhomboid major.
• It causes retraction of the scapula.
• This action is also brought about by the middle fibres of trapezius.
• The superior fibres of trapezius cause shrugging of the shoulder.
• The inferior fibres of trapezius cause rotation of the scapula for overhead abduction. This function is also done by serratus anterior.

Other Options:
Option B: Elevation of scapula is not brought about by rhomboid major. It is caused by the levator scapulae and upper fibres of trapezius.
Option C: Rotation of the scapula elevates the glenoid cavity, and not depresses it. Hence, this is an incorrect statement.
Option D: The inferior fibres of trapezius rotate the scapula tilting the glenoid cavity superiorly. This action is also brought about by serratus anterior for overhead abduction.

stem:
Lateral to medial, sequence in the floor of the 4th ventricle:
1. General visceral efferent
2. General somatic efferent
3. Special visceral efferent
4. Special somatic afferent
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Special visceral efferent (SVE): Supplies the muscles of the pharyngeal arches. Present in the nuclei of CN 5, 7, 9, 10, 11.
• General visceral efferent (GVE): Supplies the parasympathetic glands. Component of CN 3, 7, 9, 10.
• General somatic efferent (GSE): Supplies the muscles derived from the ocular and occipital somites. Component of CN 3, 4, 6, 12.
• Special visceral afferent (SVA): Carries sensations from the tongue (CN 7, 9, 10).
• General somatic afferent (GSA): Includes the trigeminal nerve and its 3 nuclei.
• Special somatic afferent (SSA): Includes nuclei of CN 1, 2, 8.
• General visceral afferent (GVA): Carried by the vagus nerve.
• In the floor of the fourth ventricle, the components are located in the following order from lateral to medial: SSA → GSA → SVE → GVE.

Other Options:
Option A: General somatic efferent supplies the muscles derived from the ocular and occipital somites. Its nerve components include CN 3, 4, 6 and 12.
Option C: General somatic afferent includes the trigeminal nerve and its 3 nuclei: mesencephalic, chief sensory and spinal.
Option D: Special visceral efferent supplies the muscles of the pharyngeal arches. It is present in the nuclei of the cranial nerves CN 5, 7, 9, 10 and 11.

stem:
Match the correct pair:
NerveFunction
1. Facial nerveA. Shrugs shoulder
2. Branch of spinal accessoryB. Taste from posterior ⅓ of the tongue
3. Glossopharyngeal nerveC. Chewing
4. Mandibular nerveD. Taste from anterior ⅔ of the tongue
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The facial nerve supplies: Muscles of the face, Sensory supply (taste) to the anterior ⅔ of tongue, Secretomotor activity to the lacrimal glands and the parotids.
• A branch of the spinal accessory nerve supplies: Sternocleidomastoid, Trapezius. Hence it is helpful in shrugging the shoulders through trapezius.
• The glossopharyngeal nerve supplies: Motor supply to the muscles of pharynx, Taste from posterior ⅓ of tongue, General sensation from posterior ⅓ of tongue.
• The mandibular nerve supplies the muscles of mastication which helps in chewing.

Other Options:
Options A, C and D: These are incorrect matches of the nerves and their functions.

stem: Which artery is involved in lateral medullary syndrome?
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The lateral medullary syndrome is caused due to a thrombosis of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery.
• It is a branch of the vertebral arteries.
• It is also known as the Wallenberg syndrome.
• It causes the following:
1. Contralateral analgesia due to involvement of the spinothalamic tract
2. Ipsilateral analgesia on face due to involvement of spinal nucleus of the trigeminal nerve
3. Ipsilateral dysphagia and dysarthria due to involvement of nucleus ambiguus

Other Options:
Option B: Anterior inferior cerebellar artery is a branch of the basilar artery. Its thrombosis does not lead to lateral medullary syndrome.
Option C: Middle cerebral artery is a terminal branch of the internal carotid artery. Its thrombosis does not lead to lateral medullary syndrome.
Option D: Anterior cerebral artery is a terminal branch of the internal carotid artery. Its thrombosis does not lead to lateral medullary syndrome.

stem:
Superior to inferior on left side, auscultation points are:
1. Pulmonary
2. Aortic
3. Mitral
4. Tricuspid
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The correct order from superior to inferior is pulmonary → aortic → tricuspid → mitral.
• Pulmonary area is auscultated in the left 2nd intercostal space near the sternal border.
• Aortic area is classically auscultated in the right 2nd intercostal space, but in this ordered list it is placed next after pulmonary as a superior auscultatory area.
• Tricuspid area is auscultated at the left lower sternal border, usually around the 4th or 5th intercostal space.
• Mitral area is auscultated at the apex beat, usually in the left 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line.
• Thus, the order from above downward is 1, 2, 4, 3.

Other Options:
Option A: 2, 3, 4, 1. This places aortic before pulmonary and pulmonary last. Pulmonary area is one of the most superior left-sided auscultatory areas. Mitral area is placed too high in this sequence. Hence, this order is incorrect.
Option C: 1, 3, 2, 4. This starts correctly with pulmonary. However, it places mitral before aortic and tricuspid. Mitral area is the most inferior and lateral auscultatory point among these. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Option D: 1, 2, 3, 4. This starts with pulmonary and aortic correctly. However, it places mitral before tricuspid. The tricuspid area lies superior/medial to the mitral area. Hence, this option is incorrect.

stem: A child with type 1 diabetes was brought in unconscious condition. Urine test was performed and the image is given below. Identify the test.
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• This is a case of diabetic ketoacidosis.
• Rothera's test is used for identification of ketone bodies.
• Rothera's test is also known as nitroprusside test.
• It is positive for: Acetone, Acetoacetate.
• Beta hydroxybutyrate, the most common ketone body, gives negative results for this test. (It is a false ketone body-containing OH group)
• In this test, a purple coloured ring of nitroprusside is formed at the junction of urine and liquor ammonia, in the presence of ammonium sulphate.

Other Options:
Option B: Biuret test is for proteins in urine. It does not test for ketone bodies.
Option C: Benedict's test is for reducing sugars. It does not test for ketone bodies. It is positive in conditions like diabetes mellitus.
Option D: Fouchet's test is for bile salts. It does not test for ketone bodies. It is positive in conditions like cholestatic jaundice.

stem: In the liver, ketone bodies are not utilised due to lack of which enzyme?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• RBCs and liver are unable to utilise energy derived from ketone bodies.
• This is due to lack of the enzyme thiophorase.
• Thiophorase converts acetoacetate to acetoacetyl CoA.
• In the process, succinyl CoA gets converted into succinate.
• Thus, thiophorase is also known as coenzyme A transferase.

Other Options:
Option A: Thioesterase is involved in fatty acid synthesis. It is required for the release of palmitic acid and stearic acid.
Option B: Thiokinase is involved in beta oxidation of fatty acids. It is required in the activation step of fatty acids.
Option C: Thiolase is involved in beta oxidation of fatty acids. It converts ketoacyl CoA into acetyl coenzyme A.

stem: In which of the following vitamin deficiency, xanthurenic acid is seen in urine?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Xanthurenic acid is seen in the urine in deficiency of vitamin B6.
• This test is also known as tryptophan load test.
• Tryptophan metabolism produces an intermediate metabolite called 3-hydroxy-kinurenine. This 3-hydroxy-kinurenine is converted into 3-hydroxy-anthranilate in the presence of the enzyme kinureninase.
• Vitamine B6 is required for this conversion.
• Therefore, in the deficiency of this vitamin, this conversion does not take place.
• 3-hydroxy-kinurenine is converted into xanthurenic acid, which is excreted in the urine.

Other Options:
Option A: Vitamin B9 deficiency can be diagnosed by histidine load test. In this test, FIGLU (formiminoglutamate) is excreted in the urine in case of deficiency of vitamin B9.
Option C: In deficiency of vitamin B12, methyl malonic acid is excreted in the urine. This deficiency manifests with megaloblastic anaemia and neurological manifestations.
Option D: RBC transketolase activity can be used to trace vitamin B1 activity. This enzyme plays a role in the HMP shunt pathway.

stem: _____ has the highest thermic effect.
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The thermogenic action of the food is also known as SDA (specific dynamic action).
• It is the energy that is required, over and above the basal metabolic rate, in order to utilise these food substances.
• This energy is utilised in the absorption and metabolism of the food.
• Proteins have the highest SDA.
• This is because they utilise energy in transamination reactions.
• The SDA of proteins is as high as 30%.
• Respiratory quotient (RQ) of proteins is 0.8.
• Energy given by proteins is 4 kCal/g.

Other Options:
Option B: Carbohydrates have an SDA ranging from 5-15%. Hence, they have a lesser SDA than proteins. RQ is 1. Energy given is 4 kCal/g.
Option C: Lipids have an SDA ranging from 0-5%. Hence, they have a lesser SDA than proteins. RQ is 0.7. Energy given is 9 kCal/g.
Option D: All of the above is not the correct option since SDA of proteins is the highest, around 30%.

stem: Deficiency of which enzyme leads to Pompe's disease?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Pompe's disease is due to deficiency of lysosomal alpha glucosidase or lysosomal acid maltase.
• It is type 2 glycogen storage disorder.
• It is also a lysosomal storage disorder.
• It affects the liver, muscles and the heart.
• Life expectancy is less than 2 years.
• Involvement of the heart in the form of cardiomyopathy.
• Other clinical features include fasting hypoglycemia and hypotonia.

Other Options:
Option A: Glucose 6 phosphatase deficiency causes Von Gierke's disease. It is characterised by episodes of fasting hypoglycemia, hyperlipidemia, hyperuricemia and hepatomegaly.
Option C: Beta glucosidase deficiency causes Gaucher's disease. It affects the bone, liver and spleen. It is characterised by pancytopenia, erlenmeyer flask deformity of the femur and presence of Gaucher's cells with crumpled tissue paper appearance in the bone marrow biopsy.
Option D: Muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency causes McArdle disease. It is characterised by exercise intolerance and second wind phenomenon and rhabdomyolysis.

stem: Regarding collagen, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: Most abundant amino acid is phenylalanine
Vitamin C and copper are needed for cross links
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• The most abundant amino acid in collagen is glycine.
• Collagen is made up of glycine-X-Y, where X and Y are proline, lysine, hydroxyproline or hydroxylysine.
• Thus glycine makes up 30% of the collagen molecule.
• The hydroxylation of proline and lysine is mediated by vitamin C dependent hydroxylase enzymes.
• Copper is the cofactor for lysyl oxidase, which is required for formation of cross links between the collagen molecules.

Other Options:
Option B: Proline and hydroxyproline are found in the collagen molecule. This is a true statement. The hydroxylation of proline and lysine is mediated by vitamin C dependent enzymes.
Option C: Type 1, 2, 3 and 5 collagen are fibrillar proteins. Hence, this is also a true statement.
Option D: Copper is the cofactor for lysyl oxidase, which is required for formation of cross links between the collagen molecules. Hence, this is a true statement.

stem: CGTTATTTACTA is the sequence on template strand in 5'-3' direction. Find the sequence in mRNA in the same direction.
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• The sequence of bases on genetic material is always read from 5' to 3' direction.
• But the template strand is always read in 3' to 5' [ATCATTTATTGC]
• After transcription of the nuclear material, the mRNA formed will be corresponding to 5' to 3' direction.
• The following are the complementary base pairs:
1. G and C
2. A and U
3. T and A
• In mRNA, thymine will be replaced by uracil.
• Thus, the correct option is UAGUAAAUAACG.

Other Options:
Option B: Option B has simply written the sequence of the DNA in the reverse, that is, 3' to 5' order. Hence, this is not the right answer.
Option C: Option C does not have the correct complementary base pairs. Hence, this is not the right answer.
Option D: Option D does not have the correct complementary base pairs. Hence, this is not the right answer.

stem: Xanthinuria is caused due to deficiency of which of the following?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Xanthine oxidase converts hypoxanthine to xanthine and then xanthine to uric acid.
• Thus, it is involved in the formation of uric acid.
• A deficiency of this enzyme will result in accumulation of xanthine and hypoxanthine.
• This will cause xanthinuria.
• The coenzymes of xanthine oxidase are:
1. Molybdenum
2. Iron
3. FAD
• A deficiency of either of these can also cause xanthinuria.

Other Options:
Option A: Magnesium is a cofactor of DNA and RNA polymerases and DNA topoisomerase 1.
Option B: Manganese is a cofactor of arginase, glutamine synthetase, pyruvate carboxylase and mitochondrial superoxide dismutase.
Option C: Zinc is contained in a variety of enzymes including carbonic anhydrase, superoxide dismutase, RNA polymerase and many of the dehydrogenases.

stem: A patient presented with muscle weakness. On investigation, cardiomyopathy was found and diagnosed as Barth syndrome. Deficiency of which of the following in mitochondrial membrane leads to Barth syndrome?
subject: Biochemistry, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Barth syndrome is an X-linked recessive disorder.
• It affects the males.
• The systems involved are:
1. Immune system
2. Skeletal muscles
3. Heart muscles
4. Growth
• Cardiomyopathy is characteristic of this condition.
• In this condition, there is decreased synthesis of cardiolipin, which is the most important component of the inner mitochondrial membrane.
• It confers antigenicity to the mitochondrial membrane.

Other Options:
Option B: Plasmalogens play a crucial role as endogenous antioxidants, protecting other PL, lipid and lipoprotein particles from oxidative stress. They are a subclass of cell membrane glycerophospholipids.
Option C: Lecithin is involved in foetal lung maturity and formation of surfactants. Premature infants suffer from respiratory distress syndrome if surfactant is not adequately formed.
Option D: Lysolecithin is primarily used as an emulsifier alongside other fatty acids, vitamins, plant extracts, and glycols. It is also known as lysophosphatidyl choline.

stem: Which hormone acts through the JAK-STAT pathway?
subject: Physiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• JAK-STAT stands for Janus kinase/signal transducers and activators of transcription. It is a signalling pathway.
• The following hormones act through the JAK-STAT pathway:
1. Growth hormone
2. Prolactin
3. Leptin
4. Cytokines
5. Interferons
• Activation of JAK-STAT results in dimerisation of the receptor.
• This is followed by autophosphorylation.
• After this step, a new protein is transcribed, which acts as a second messenger.

Other Options:
Option A: Calcitonin acts through cAMP. It is a water soluble hormone with a cell surface receptor. It is secreted by the parafollicular C cells of thyroid gland.
Option C: Aldosterone is a mineralocorticoid. It is secreted by the zona glomerulosa of the adrenal cortex. It has intracellular receptors.
Option D: Vasopressin acts through cAMP and IP3-DAG pathway. It is secreted by the hypothalamus through the posterior pituitary.

stem: Defect in which of the following leads to cholestasis?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• MRP 2 is responsible for the active transport of conjugated bilirubin into bile canaliculi.
• This is the rate limiting step in the transport of bilirubin.
• A mutation in this protein causes cholestasis.
• It is responsible for Dubin Johnson syndrome.
• Dubin Johnson syndrome is characterised by neonatal cholestasis and a black liver.

Other Options:
Option A: ABCA is defective in Tangier's disease. This is characterised by orange coloured tonsils. ABCA is responsible for transport of cholesterol from peripheral cells to HDL.
Option C: Some bilirubin escapes transport into bile canaliculi by MRP2. MRP3 is responsible for transporting the escaped bilirubin into portal circulation.
Option D: OAT 1b1/ 1b3 is responsible for transporting the conjugated bilirubin into the hepatocytes. It is defective in Rotor syndrome.

stem:
Alteration in DNA sequence can be assessed by
1. FISH
2. Pyrosequencing
3. RFLP
4. Microarray
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Sequencing is the gold standard for detection of the alterations in DNA sequence.
• Sanger's sequencing is first generation sequencing. It is based upon a chain termination method using ddNTP.
• Pyrosequencing is a second generation sequencing technique. It is best applied on replicating nuclear material. The pyrophosphates released are detected by visible light.
• Restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP) is a sequencing method based on restriction site and the number of fragments generated after using restriction enzymes.
• Microarray is a large-scale sequencing method. In this, oligonucleotide probes are placed on chips and are used to detect particular DNA sequences.
• Therefore, Pyrosequencing, RFLP, and Microarray can assess alterations in DNA sequence.

Other Options:
Options A, C, and D: FISH stands for fluorescent in situ hybridization. It is a laboratory technique for detecting and locating a specific DNA sequence on a chromosome, but it is not typically used for reading exact base-pair alterations like the sequencing methods listed.

stem: A patient was admitted in the emergency ward with respiratory discomfort and was found to have carbon monoxide poisoning. Which among the following could be expected in this patient?
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In carbon monoxide poisoning, anaemic hypoxia is seen.
• This is because carbon monoxide displaces oxygen from haemoglobin.
• Thus, the oxygen saturation of haemoglobin decreases, as shown in the graph.
• However, PaO2 remains normal.
• This is because PaO2 is determined by the free form of oxygen and not by the form bound to haemoglobin.
• Since the free concentration of oxygen remains unchanged in carbon monoxide poisoning, the PaO2 remains normal.

Other Options:
Option B: Histotoxic hypoxia is a form of hypoxia in which the cellular respiration is compromised due to a block in the enzymatic pathways. Cyanide poisoning classically causes histotoxic hypoxia.
Option C: Hypoxic hypoxia is seen in cases where oxygen concentration in the atmosphere is decreased. It appears in high altitudes and in cases of death due to suffocation.
Option D: Stagnant hypoxia occurs due to failure of the circulatory mechanism of the body. It is seen in patients of cardiac failure.

stem: Which among the following will stimulate the taste receptors associated with bitter taste sensations on the tongue?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Bitter taste can be stimulated by a variety of substances. Some of these include:
1. Poisons
2. Alkaloids
3. Quinine
4. Strychnine etc.
• Sweet taste is stimulated by saccharides.
• Salty taste is stimulated by sodium chloride.
• Sour taste is stimulated by protons (H+ ions).
• Umami taste is stimulated by glutamate.

Other Options:
Option A: Amino acids do not stimulate bitter taste in particular. They can stimulate a variety of tastes like sweet, umami, bitter or salty based on their electrochemical properties.
Option B: Aldehydes do not stimulate bitter taste. Aldehydes have a malty type of flavour.
Option C: Protons (H+ ions) do not stimulate bitter taste. They stimulate sour taste.

stem: The Windkessel effect will be exhibited by all the following, except
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Windkessel effect is shown by the aorta and its large branches.
• Windkessel effect refers to the phenomenon where in systole there is dilatation of the vessels and in diastole there is elastic recoil of the vessels, which pushes the blood forwards.
• This is responsible for the diastolic notch in the pulse waveform.
• Radial artery is a peripheral artery, which arises from the brachial artery.
• Hence, it does not show the Windkessel effect.

Other Options:
Option A: Carotid artery is a direct branch of the arch of aorta or the brachiocephalic trunk. Thus, it shows the Windkessel effect.
Option C: Aorta and its major branches characteristically show the Windkessel effect. Thus, it is not the answer.
Option D: Renal artery is a direct branch of the abdominal aorta. Thus, it shows the Windkessel effect.

stem: A patient was diagnosed to have cystic fibrosis. Which of the following channels would be defective in this patient?
subject: Pathology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Cystic fibrosis is caused due to a mutation in the CFTR gene.
• The CFTR gene is located on chromosome 7q.
• CFTR is a transmembrane protein which regulates the transport of chloride channels.
• Hence, in patients of cystic fibrosis, the concentrations of chloride will be deranged.
• The CFTR protein belongs to the ABC protein group.
• ABC proteins stand for ATP binding cassette proteins.
• These include:
1. Multidrug resistant receptor (MDR)
2. Sulfonylurea receptor on beta cells of pancreas (SUR)
3. CFTR protein

Other Options:
Option A: Calcium ion concentrations are not deranged in cystic fibrosis. 8.5 to 10.5 mg/dL is the normal calcium levels in the blood.
Option B: Sodium ion concentrations are not deranged in cystic fibrosis. 135 to 145 mmol/L is the normal sodium ion concentrations in the blood.
Option C: Potassium ion concentrations are not deranged in cystic fibrosis. 3.5 to 5.0 mmol/L is the normal potassium ion concentration in the blood.

stem: All the following gastrointestinal hormones and their sources are correctly matched, except:
options: Secretin - S cells of duodenum & jejunum
Motilin - M cells of duodenum & jejunum
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Motilin is secreted by the Mo cells of the upper small intestine.
• M cells are present in the Peyer's patches and other lymphoid tissue of the small intestine.
• M cells do not secrete motilin.
• Though some authors use Mo cells and M cells interchangeably, this is the best option from the given options.

Other Options:
Option A: Secretin is secreted by the S cells of the duodenum and jejunum. Secretin has 3 main functions: regulation of Pancreatic bicarbonate secretion, regulation of gastric acid and osmoregulation.
Option B: Cholecystokinin is secreted by the I cells of duodenum. It helps in increasing contractions of the gallbladder and also release of the pancreatic enzymes.
Option D: Gastrin is secreted by the parietal G cells of the stomach. It increases the HCl secretion by the stomach, which further increases acidity of the stomach.

stem: Which among the following amino acids is not involved in the synthesis of hormones?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Glycine is not involved in the synthesis of hormones.
• Glycine acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system.
• Tryptophan is used in the synthesis of hormone melatonin and serotonin.
• Tyrosine is used to synthesise catecholamines like dopamine, epinephrine and norepinephrine. Tyrosine is also iodinated to form the thyroid hormones.
• Phenylalanine forms tyrosine and is hence indirectly used to synthesise catecholamines like dopamine, epinephrine and norepinephrine. Tyrosine is also iodinated to form the thyroid hormones.

Other Options:
Option A: Tryptophan is used in the synthesis of hormone melatonin and serotonin.
Option C: Phenylalanine forms tyrosine and is hence indirectly used to synthesise catecholamines like dopamine, epinephrine and norepinephrine. Tyrosine is also iodinated to form the thyroid hormones.
Option D: Tyrosine is used to synthesise catecholamines like dopamine, epinephrine and norepinephrine. Tyrosine is also iodinated to form the thyroid hormones.

stem:
Regarding the skeletal muscle contraction, identify the correct statements from the following
1. Calcium is released primarily from the mitochondria
2. The extracellular calcium is not vital for muscle contraction
3. In EC coupling, the sarcolemmal voltage gated calcium channels have no role
4. Calcium is released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
The following steps take place in excitation contraction coupling (EC coupling):
• 1. Action potential is generated at the neuromuscular junction.
• 2. Action potential is transmitted along the sarcolemma and down the T tubule.
• 3. Action potential triggers calcium release from the terminal cisternae of the T tubules.
• 4. Calcium ions bind to troponin.
• 5. Action of tropomyosin blocked.
• 6. Actin sites exposed, which attach to myosin.
• 7. Muscle contracts.
• 8. Calcium ions are removed by active transport after action potential terminates.
• 9. Tropomyosin blockage is restored and contraction of muscle ends.

Statement 2 is correct as extracellular calcium is not essential for skeletal muscle contraction. Calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum brings about muscle contraction. In smooth muscles and cardiac muscles, extracellular calcium plays a role.

The following image depicts the coupling of the muscle action potential to Ca++ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. The dihydropyridine receptor interacts with the ryanodine receptor, which results in release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

The released calcium ions further activate the release of more calcium from intracellular stores like the sarcoplasmic reticulum, and this is known as calcium induced calcium release.

Other Options:
Option A: Option 1 is incorrect as calcium ions are released from sarcoplasmic reticulum. They are toxic to the mitochondria and hence are never released from the mitochondria.
Option C: In EC coupling, the sarcolemmal voltage gated calcium channels have an important role, ie, to release calcium ions. Hence statement 3 is incorrect.
Option D: In EC coupling, the sarcolemmal voltage gated calcium channels have an important role, ie, to release calcium ions. Hence statement 3 is incorrect.

stem: In the radial artery, the changes in blood pressure were studied. In the Valsalva maneuver, initially the blood pressure was found to be elevated. This could be explained by
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Valsalva maneuver is the performance of forced expiration against a closed glottis.
• On forceful inspiration, more volume of blood is sucked into the thoracic cavity and into the right heart.
• On forceful expiration, as in valsalva maneuver, more volume of blood is ejected out of the left ventricle.
• This builds up increased pressure in the aorta.
• This increased aortic pressure is transmitted to the peripheral arteries of the body.

Other Options:
Option A: In Valsalva maneuver, there is definitely an increase in left ventricular pressure. However, this is not directly linked to the increase in peripheral vascular pressure. Thus, this is not the right answer.
Option C: During Valsalva maneuver, left ventricular volume decreases due to forceful expiration. Hence, this is not the correct answer.
Option D: None of the above is not the correct answer, as option B is right.

stem:
The following are the components of a sarcomere. Arrange them based on their location from the periphery to the centre of the sarcomere
1. H zone
2. M line
3. A band
4. Z line
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• A sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of a myocyte.
• A sarcomere is composed of two main protein filaments: thin and thick filaments.
• The thick filaments refer to the myosin filaments.
• The thin filaments refer to the actin filaments.
• As can be interpreted from the structure, the order from periphery to the centre is:
1. Z line
2. A band
3. H zone
4. M line
• The Z line depicts the demarcation between two sarcomeres.
• The A band is the dark band of the muscle sarcomere that corresponds to the thick myosin filaments.
• The H zone is the central part of the thick filaments (A band) that is not overlapped by thin filaments (actin).
• The M line contains myomesin and it marks the centre of the sarcomere.

Other Options:
Options A, C and D: These are not the correct orders of arrangement from periphery to the centre, as explained above.

stem:
Arrange the structures of the auditory pathway in the sequential order of sound transmission:
1. Superior olivary complex
2. Inferior colliculus
3. Auditory cortex
4. Lateral lemniscus
5. Medial geniculate body
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Course of auditory pathways is as follows:
a) Superior olivary complex
b) Nucleus of the lateral lemniscus
c) Inferior colliculus
d) Medial geniculate body
e) Auditory cortex
• Trick to remember: ECOLIMA
E - Eighth nerve
C - Cochlear nuclei
O - Olivary complex
L - Lateral lemniscus
I - Inferior colliculus
M - Medial geniculate body
A - Auditory cortex.

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: All the other options consist of incorrect sequences.

stem: At which of the following lung volumes, the relaxation pressure will be zero?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Relaxation pressure will be zero at the end of quiet expiration.
• The transpulmonary pressure is minimum.
• At this point, the lung is at equilibrium.
• This is because the outward pull on the thorax is balanced by the inward recoil of the lungs.
• Hence the equal and opposite forces cancel out, thereby bringing the lung-thorax system to an equilibrium.
• At the end of quiet expiration, the lung volume corresponds to the functional residual capacity (FRC).

Other Options:
Option A: Residual volume is the volume of air remaining in the lungs at the end of a forceful expiration.
Option B: Total lung capacity is the total volume of air the lungs can hold after maximum forceful inspiration.
Option C: Inspiration capacity refers to the sum of inspiratory reserve volume and tidal volume. It is the amount of air that can be maximally inhaled during a forceful inspiration.

stem: Which among the following will be synthesised from the smooth endoplasmic reticulum?
subject: Physiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Steroid hormones are synthesised from the smooth endoplasmic reticulum.
• Steroid hormones are lipid soluble hormones.
• They have intracellular receptors.
• Their receptors may be intracytoplasmic or intranuclear.
• The examples of steroid hormones include glucocorticoids (cortisol), mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) and sex steroids (testosterone, estrogen).

Other Options:
Option A: Peptide hormones constitute the majority of the hormones including the hypothalamic and pituitary hormones, angiotensin, CCK, gastrin etc.
Option C: Amino acid derivatives include epinephrine, norepinephrine and thyroid hormones synthesised from tyrosine, and serotonin and melatonin synthesised from tryptophan.
Option D: Glycopeptide hormones include hCG, luteinizing hormone (LH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH).

stem: A patient was started on a drug for mood disorder and later on he developed priapism. What is the most likely causative drug?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• All four drugs are antidepressants.
• Trazodone is known for causing Priapism.
• The drug of choice for depression is selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI). Eg. Fluoxetine.
• In case of resistance to SSRI, it can be treated by serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI). Eg. Venlafaxine.
• Atypical antidepressants like Trazodone, Mirtazapine, Bupropion can be used when SNRI fail to give desirable outcomes.
• Trazodone causes painful penile erection called priapism.

Other Options:
Option A: Mirtazapine is a noradrenaline and selective serotonin antagonist, causing sedation as side effect.
Option C: Venlafaxine is an SNRI causing erectile dysfunction as side effect.
Option D: Bupropion is a norepinephrine dopamine reuptake inhibitor used as an anti smoking drug.

stem: Which of the following is true about the therapeutic index of a drug?
options: Denotes safety and efficacy in population
Denotes safety and efficacy in individual
Ratio of median lethal dose in 50% of population to median effective dose in 50% of population
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Therapeutic Index is defined as the ratio of LD50 and TD50.
• TD50 is the therapeutic dose of a drug for 50% of the population.
• LD50 is the lethal dose of a drug for 50% of the population.
• The therapeutic index denotes safety and effectiveness of drug in population.
• Ratio of median lethal dose in 50% of population to median effective dose in 50% of population. This statement is correct.

Other Options:
Option A: Denotes safety and efficacy in population - this is false as therapeutic index doesn't account for efficacy.
Option B: Denotes safety and efficacy in individuals - this is false as therapeutic index doesn't account for the individual.
Option D: None of the above - this is not the correct answer as C is true.

stem: Which Graph is used for vancomycin dosing?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Vancomycin acts by inhibiting the cell wall synthesis of the bacteria.
• The graph used for the monitoring or dosing of any drug is called a nomogram.
• Thus, option C is correct.
• The name of this nomogram chart can differ based on the drug.
• For vancomycin, it is called Duke University Nomogram.
• For paracetamol, it is called Rumack Matthew Nomogram.

Other Options:
Options A, B and D: Halstead, Salazar and Kitsco are not associated with vancomycin dosing.

stem: Which is true about keynote 189 trial?
options: Nivolumab plus chemotherapy used in NSCLC
Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy used in NSCLC
subject: Pharmacology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• There have been multiple keynote trials which deal with Pembrolizumab.
• It is a PD-1 blocker, thus acting as an immune checkpoint inhibitor.
• Most commonly, keynote trials have been done on Non Small Cell Lung cancer (NSCLC).
• In every clinical trial, we do not use the new drug as monotherapy. It is added along with the original chemotherapy.
• Thus, option D is correct.

Other Options:
Option A: Nivolumab is used in NSCLC. Nivolumab was not involved in the keynote trials.
Option B: Nivolumab plus chemotherapy used in NSCLC. Nivolumab was not involved in the keynote trials.
Option C: Pembrolizumab used in NSCLC. Pembrolizumab was not involved as monotherapy in the keynote trials.

stem: Recently approved drug by FDA for treatment of RETT's Syndrome?
subject: Pharmacology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental condition seen in young children.
• It is associated with impaired motor activity and repetitive hand movements.
• Trofinetide is a newly approved drug for the treatment of Rett syndrome.
• IGF-1 is a neuroprotective agent.
• The tripeptide of IGF-1 is called Trofinetide.

Other Options:
Option A: Abacavir is an antiretroviral drug.
Options B and D: Cltazimumab and Sinconimabide are not associated with the management of Rett syndrome (and they are looking like random names).

stem: Which Drug is used for trastuzumab resistant Her-2 positive breast cancer?
subject: Pharmacology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Trastuzumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks Her2/neu.
• In case of its resistance, Lapatinib is used.
• It blocks Her-1 and Her-2 receptors.
• Any drug ending with -nib are tyrosine kinase receptor inhibitors.
• Sorafenib blocks VEGFR tyrosine kinase. It is used in gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST) and renal cell carcinoma (RCC).
• Vemurafenib blocks BRAF receptor and can be used in melanoma.
• Erlotinib blocks EGFR receptor and can be used in non small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and pancreatic cancer.

Other Options:
Option B: Sorafenib blocks VEGFR tyrosine kinase. It is used in gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST) and renal cell carcinoma (RCC).
Option C: Vemurafenib blocks BRAF receptor and can be used in melanoma.
Option D: Erlotinib blocks EGFR receptor and can be used in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and pancreatic cancer.

stem: A diabetic patient was started on a drug that decreased her HbA1c from 7.4 to 6.8, after a few weeks she started complaining of itching around the vulvovaginal area. What is the most likely drug she started?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Canagliflozin is an SGLT-2 inhibitor, as it ends with the suffix -gliflozin.
• They inhibit reabsorption of sodium and glucose in the PCT of nephron.
• Thus, there is glycosuria.
• This leads to infections in the urinary tract, most commonly by Candida.
• They also increase the risk of Fournier's gangrene, diabetic ketoacidosis and fractures.
• Due to sodium excretion effect they are also approved for chronic heart failure.

Other Options:
Option B: Acarbose is an alpha glucosidase inhibitor and it does not cause urogenital infection or candidiasis.
Option C: Linagliptin is a sulfonylurea and it does not cause urogenital infection or candidiasis.
Option D: Metformin does not cause urogenital infection or candidiasis.

stem: Which of the following is true about "LITHIUM"?
options: Fine tremors are seen at therapeutic blood levels
Lithium is not absorbed from the gut
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Lithium is a drug approved for bipolar disorder.
• It is absorbed from the gut.
• Therapeutic drug monitoring needs to be done with lithium.
• It can cause fine tremors even at therapeutic blood levels.
• In higher doses, it can cause coarse tremors.
• It can also cause diabetes insipidus or thyroid anomalies.
• It causes Ebstein's anomaly in the foetus, if taken in pregnancy.

Other Options:
Option B: Lithium is not absorbed from the gut - this is incorrect as lithium has almost 100% intestinal absorption.
Option C: It does not cause teratogenicity - this is incorrect, as it causes Ebstein's anomaly in the foetus, if taken in pregnancy.
Option D: Approved for absence seizure - this is incorrect, as it is not approved for seizures.

stem:
Match the following?
DrugReceptors
1. Mirabegrona. Beta 1
2. Indacaterolb. Beta 1 & 2
3. Betaxololc. Beta 3
4. Isoproterenold. Beta 2
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Mirabegron is a beta 3 agonist used in overactive bladder.
• Indacaterol is a LABA (long acting beta 2 agonist) used in asthma.
• Betaxolol is a cardioselective beta blocker, thus it blocks beta 1 only.
• It is mainly used in the treatment of open-angle glaucoma, essential hypertension, post-myocardial infarction and chronic stable angina.
• Isoproterenol is a beta 1 and beta 2 agonist. It has limited clinical use.
• Isoproterenol is indicated to treat mild or transient episodes of heart block not requiring electric shock or pacemakers and cardiac arrest from heart block, when pacemaker therapy is unavailable.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect matching.
Option B: Incorrect matching.
Option C: Incorrect matching.

stem: A patient with Schizophrenia is being treated with clozapine, what is the rare side effect of this drug which should be monitored in this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Clozapine as an atypical antipsychotic drug which is given in resistant cases of schizophrenia.
• It can cause rapid reduction in neutrophil count which is termed as agranulocytosis.
• This is an emergency condition, thus neutrophil count should be regularly done, when clozapine is prescribed.
• Myocarditis is another rare side effect of clozapine but doesn't require monitoring.
• Other side effects include wet pillow syndrome, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus and sedation.


Other Options:
Option B: Intracerebral bleeding is not a side effect of clozapine.
Option C: Myocardial infarction can be a rare side effect of clozapine. But, its monitoring is not routinely done.
Option D: Suicidal tendencies is not seen with clozapine. It may be seen with bupropion.

stem: Which of the following oral antidiabetic drugs causes an increase in GIP and GLP-1 level and decrease in HbA1C levels?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• GIP stands for gastric inhibitory polypeptide, which is also known as glucose dependent insulinotropic peptide.
• GLP-1 stands for glucagon like peptide-1.
• These two molecules increase insulin levels in the body.
• Among the options, Alogliptin is the only drug which causes an increase in GIP and GLP-1 levels in the body and decrease HbA1C levels.
• Alogliptin is a DPP-4 inhibitor, which works by preventing the breakdown of incretin hormones like GIP and GLP-1, leading to increased levels of these hormones that stimulate insulin secretion and ultimately lower blood sugar levels (HbA1C).


Other Options:
Option A: Liraglutide is a GLP-1 agonist and can be used in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Option B: Pramlintide is an amylin analogue and can be used in the management of type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Option D: Metformin is a biguanide, which decreases the blood glucose levels and increases the insulin sensitivity.

stem: A patient was given morphine during surgery and he developed respiratory depression. Which drug should be used as an antidote?
subject: Pharmacology, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• This is a case of opioid poisoning.
• Opioids like morphine act on the mu receptor and can cause respiratory depression.
• Other clinical features of opioid poisoning include:
• 1. Pinpoint pupils
• 2. Drowsiness
• 3. Disorientation
• 4. Vomiting
• 5. Peripheral cyanosis
• 6. Cold clammy skin
• 7. Muscle spasms
• The antidote of opioid poisoning is naloxone.
• It is given intravenously and is a fast acting medication, which can reverse the effects of opioid overdose.


Other Options:
Option B: Pethidine is an opioid agonist, thus worsens the symptoms.
Option C: Fomepizole is an alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor used in methanol poisoning.
Option D: Naltrexone is an oral drug and is slow to act, thus not preferred.

stem: Which of the following is responsible for teratogenicity due to thalidomide?
options: Inhibition of IL2, IL4 and IL5
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Thalidomide is a drug which causes teratogenic effect in foetus.
• It can cause phocomelia (short limbs) or seal like limbs.
• This is mediated by its binding to cereblon.
• Thalidomide inhibits cell growth and has anti inflammatory property.
• It is given in tumours like myeloma and also in type 2 lepra reaction.
• It binds to cereblon, a ligase enzyme, which causes teratogenicity.


Other Options:
Option A: Free radical mediated DNA damage is a feature of thalidomide, accounting for its anticancer property.
Option B: Suppression of angiogenesis is a feature of thalidomide, accounting for its anticancer property.
Option D: Inhibition of IL-2, IL-4 and IL-5 is a feature of thalidomide, accounting for its anti-inflammatory property.

stem: Osimertinib is used in NSCLC with mutation due to?
subject: Pharmacology, Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Non Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) is associated with an EGFR mutation, leading to tyrosine kinase overactivation.
• L858R mutation is the most common mutation associated with EGFR.
• For this mutation, Erlotinib is used.
• However, sometimes there can be T790M mutation.
• For this mutation, Osimertinib is used.
• Thus, option A is the correct option.


Other Options:
Option B: L858R mutation is not treated by Osimertinib. It is treated by Erlotinib.
Option C: Only T790M mutations (not T890M) are treated by Osimertinib.
Option D: Only T790M mutations (not M790T mutations) are treated by Osimertinib.

stem: A village kid goes to a temple with his grandmother. He was bitten by some insect and started crying due to extreme degree of pain. After some time, she noticed that the child was sweating profusely and hands were becoming cold. He vomited a couple of times. The mother immediately rushed him to the emergency services. His pulse was 150/minute and her BP 90/60 mmHg. Sustained priapism for 7 hours. Treatment is?
subject: Pharmacology, FMT, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The clinical stem is likely to be a diagnosis of scorpion bite leading to priapism and increased sympathetic activity.
• In this case anti-venom is the first line of treatment.
• If there is hypertension in scorpion bite, then the drug of choice is prazosin.
• Prazosin can also cause priapism. Thus, if the patient already has priapism, then prazosin is avoided.
• If the patient is in shock, then adrenaline is given.
• Thus, option A is the best answer.


Other Options:
Option B: Prazosin is given for hypertension in scorpion bite cases.
Option C: Adrenaline is given in scorpion bite with features of shock.
Option D: Methylprednisolone has no role in scorpion bite.

stem:
During an agglutination test, serial dilutions are performed. What is the reason for results in points 1-3?
1. 1:1 - Negative
2. 1:4 - Negative
3. 1:8 - Negative
4. 1:16 - Positive
5. 1:32 - Positive
subject: Microbiology, Immunology
explanation:
Solution
• Serology involves study of serum to check for the presence of antigen or antibody.
• For example, a patient suffering from an infection produces antibody against that organism in his body as a part of immunological reaction.
• We take the serum sample and add the antigen to this.
• Sometimes, the antibody produced in the body is much more than the antigen that is added.
• In initial dilutions (points 1-3) there is antibody excess, which leads to negative test results. This is called the Prozone phenomenon.
• To prevent such false-negative results due to prozone phenomenon, serial dilutions are done so that you can get equal amounts of antigen and antibody (lattice hypothesis).


Other Options:
Option A: Antigen excess is when the added antigen is in excess. It is called the postzone phenomenon.
Option C: Improper reagent is not an appropriate explanation for this phenomenon.
Option D: Invalid test is not an appropriate explanation for this phenomenon.

stem: Which of the following is false about rubella?
options: Rubella spreads when an infected person coughs or sneezes.
A person with rubella may spread the disease to others up to one week before the rash appears, and remain contagious up to 7 days after.
One dose of the MMR vaccine is about 50% effective at preventing rubella.
Most common birth defects from CRS (congenital rubella syndrome) can include: deafness, cataracts, heart defects, intellectual disabilities.
subject: Microbiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• One dose of the MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) vaccine is about 90% effective at preventing rubella. The efficacy of two doses is around 99%.
• Rubella is only transmitted transplacentally, and is unlikely to be transmitted perinatally.
• If transplacental transmission takes place in the first trimester, it can lead to congenital rubella syndrome. This includes deafness, cataracts, heart defects and intellectual disabilities.
• Rubella spreads when an infected person coughs or sneezes. A person with rubella may spread the disease to others up to one week before the rash appears, and remain contagious up to 7 days after. This is why people with rubella are advised to avoid contact with others for one week after rash disappears.


Other Options:
Option A: Rubella spreads when an infected person coughs or sneezes. This is true as rubella spreads by droplet infection.
Option B: A person with rubella may spread the disease to others up to one week before the rash appears, and remain contagious up to 7 days after. This is true.
Option D: Most common birth defects from CRS (congenital rubella syndrome) can include deafness, cataracts, heart defects and intellectual disabilities.

stem: An elderly diabetic patient with immunosuppressive state presented with a skin lesion, biopsy showed gram-positive oval budding yeast cells with pseudohyphae. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The clinical features of immunosuppressed state with the typical morphology describing oval budding yeast cells with pseudohyphae is characteristic of Candida albicans.
• Candida is also more likely to infect immunocompromised individuals.
• These include the elderly, diabetics and people taking immunosuppressants.


Other Options:
Option A: Invasive aspergillosis - Aspergillus shows acute angled branching or dichotomous branching with septa. It presents as pulmonary lesions, aspergilloma or invasive lesions in transplant patients.
Option C: Invasive mucormycosis - Mucor shows non septate hyphae with right angled branching. It is also more common among diabetics, renal transplant patients and post covid patients.
Option D: Invasive histoplasmosis - Histoplasma doesn't show pseudohyphae. It is a dimorphic fungus and mostly seen as opportunistic infections in AIDS.

stem: Bacterial cell wall with pilus and capsule given in the image below, what is the most likely organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows periplasmic space which is seen in Gram negative organisms.
• It also shows lipooligosaccharide (endotoxin) and capsulated organism which is typical for Neisseria. Thus, Neisseria is the most likely organism in the picture.
• Neisseria meningitidis is a gram-negative coccus responsible for causing meningitis. It is a capsulated organism.
• It causes the following diseases:
• 1. Meningitis
• 2. Meningococcemia, with or without meningitis (characterised by fever, headache, vomiting and musculoskeletal pain)
• 3. Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome

Other Options:
Option A: Staphylococcus is a gram-positive coccus, it does not have capsule.
Option C: E. coli is a gram-negative bacillus but it doesn't have lipooligosaccharide. It has lipopolysaccharide.
Option D: Pseudomonas is a gram-negative bacillus that doesn't contain lipooligosaccharide. It has lipopolysaccharide.

stem: The faeces of cats are generally very carefully disposed off to prevent the transmission of toxoplasmosis. In which form is toxoplasmosis transmitted via cat faeces?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Toxoplasmosis is caused by Toxoplasma gondii.
• It is a non-motile protozoan unlike most other protozoa.
• The definitive host is cat.
• The intermediate host is mouse usually.
• Man is an accidental intermediate host.
• The protozoan is transmitted via intake of oocyst excreted in cat faeces.
• It can be transmitted via intake of bradyzoites present in tissue cysts present in muscles of infected animals.
• It can also be transmitted via tachyzoites via blood transfusion or transplacental transmission.

Other Options:
Option A: Bradyzoite - It can be transmitted via bradyzoites in tissue like muscles.
Option C: Tachyzoite - It can be transmitted via tachyzoites when there is blood or transplacental transmission.
Option D: Gametocytes are not seen in cat faeces.

stem: Identify the disease caused by the organism shown in image below.
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows hyphae having acute-angled branching filaments.
• This is characteristic for Aspergillus which shows acute angled branching or dichotomous branching.
• It can cause a range of fungal infections, like aspergilloma, allergic aspergillosis and invasive fungal aspergillosis.
• There are 3 main species that are medically pathogenic to humans: Aspergillus flavus, Aspergillus fumigatus and Aspergillus niger.

Other Options:
Option A: Mucormycosis is caused by fungi belonging to order mucorales, like Rhizopus and Mucor. It is an angioinvasive fungus that mainly affects immunocompromised individuals. Uncontrolled diabetic state and increased iron concentrations boost the growth of the fungus. Mucor is non septate with right angled branching.
Option C: Histoplasmosis is caused by fungus Histoplasma, present chiefly in the bird and bat droppings. It mainly affects the lungs and also causes joint pain and rash. It is a dimorphic fungus.
Option D: Blastomycosis is a fungal infection caused by Blastomyces dermatitidis. It presents as a pulmonary infection after the inhalation of spores. It is also a dimorphic fungus.

stem: What is the diagnosis based on the given image?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows non-septate hyphae with right angled branching.
• Thus it is likely to be Mucor.
• Mucormycosis is caused by fungi belonging to order Mucorales, like Rhizopus and Mucor.
• It is an angioinvasive fungus that mainly affects immunocompromised individuals.
• Uncontrolled diabetic state and increased iron concentrations boost the growth of the fungus.

Other Options:
Option A: Aspergillus shows acute angled branching or dichotomous branching with septa. It can cause infections like aspergilloma, allergic aspergillosis and invasive fungal aspergillosis. The three main species pathogenic to humans are Aspergillus flavus, Aspergillus fumigatus and Aspergillus niger.
Option C: Nocardiosis is caused by bacteria Nocardia. It is a gram positive, catalase-positive filamentous bacterium. It is partially acid-fast. It forms branching filaments. It does not show non-septate hyphae with right angled branching.
Option D: Fusariosis is mainly seen in neutropenic individuals. Fusarium is a filamentous fungus present in the soil. It does not show non-septate hyphae with right angled branching. Fusarium has fusiform or spindle-shaped cells.

stem: A 6-week-old neonate presented with sepsis. The blood culture showed Gram-positive bacilli with motility. Which of the following is correct?
options: Listeria - Tumbling motility (motile at 22 degree and non-motile at 37 degree)
Listeria - Tumbling motility (motile at 37 degree and non-motile at 22 degree)
Proteus - Swarming Motility (motile at 37 degree and non-motile at 22 degree)
Proteus - Swarming Motility (motile at 22 degree and non-motile at 37 degree)
subject: Microbiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Listeria monocytogenes is the correct organism because it is a Gram-positive bacillus causing neonatal sepsis and shows tumbling motility.
• Listeria monocytogenes is a Gram-positive bacillus.
• It is an important cause of neonatal sepsis and meningitis.
• It shows characteristic tumbling motility.
• Motility is best demonstrated at room temperature, around 22-25°C.
• It is usually non-motile or less motile at 37°C.
• Therefore, the correct statement is Listeria – tumbling motility, motile at 22°C and non-motile at 37°C.

Other Options:
Option B: Listeria and tumbling motility are correctly matched. However, the temperature pattern is reversed. Listeria is more motile at 22–25°C. It is less motile or non-motile at 37°C.
Option C: Proteus shows swarming motility. However, Proteus is a Gram-negative bacillus, not Gram-positive. It is not the typical cause suggested by neonatal sepsis with Gram-positive motile bacilli. The clinical and microbiological clues point to Listeria. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Option D: Proteus is not a Gram-positive bacillus. It shows swarming motility, but it does not fit the blood culture description. The temperature pattern given is not the key feature here. Neonatal sepsis with Gram-positive motile bacilli suggests Listeria monocytogenes. Hence, this option is incorrect.

stem: Protozoa seen inside the Kupffer cell of the liver?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Kupffer cells are the macrophages of the liver.
• The protozoa seen inside these cells is Leishmania donovani.
• It causes visceral leishmaniasis called Kala Azar.
• It can also cause cutaneous leishmaniasis, which may present with different symptoms based on the organism.
• Plasmodium infects hepatocytes.
• Babesia usually does not infect the liver cells.

Other Options:
Option A: Plasmodium infects the erythrocytes and hepatocytes, not the Kupffer cells and presents with erythrocytic cycle or exoerythrocytic cycle depending upon the species.
Option C: Babesia does not infect the Kupffer cells. It infects the erythrocytes.
Option D: Toxoplasmosis does not infect the Kupffer cells. It presents as tissue cysts and infect the muscle, brain and REC.

stem: Critical care specialists have grown organisms in different cultures and observed resistance to carbapenem, aminoglycosides and susceptible to cotrimoxazole and Ticarcillin and clavulanic acid. What is the most likely organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Some organisms are intrinsically resistant to certain antibiotics while helps in preliminary identification of the organism.
• This clinical stem describes an organism which shows resistance to several antibiotics including carbapenem and aminoglycosides.
• The organism which fits this description is Stenotrophomonas maltophila.
• It is susceptible to cotrimoxazole and Ticarcillin and clavulanic acid.

Other Options:
Option B: Burkholderia can be treated by cotrimoxazole and meropenem, so it is ruled out.
Option C: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is sensitive to aminoglycosides like gentamicin and amikacin.
Option D: Acinetobacter baumannii can be resistant to ampicillin, amoxicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, ertapenem, aztreonam, chloramphenicol and fosfomycin.

stem: An adult female presented with foul-smelling discharge per vagina, pap smear image is given below. What is the correct description of appearance shown on the pap smear?
options: Epithelial cells with coco-bacilli stuck on the surface
subject: Microbiology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows a clue cell, which is characteristic of Bacterial Vaginosis.
• It is due to the increase of the organism Gardnerella vaginalis and decrease of Lactobacillus.
• Clue cells are the vaginal epithelial cells, which are studded with the bacilli on the surface as shown in the picture.
• This disease is characterised by foul smelling grey discharge per vaginum.
• The vaginal pH is more than 4.5.
• Whiff test is positive in bacterial vaginosis.

Other Options:
Option B: Macrophage with engulfed bacteria - this is incorrect as clue cells are vaginal epithelial cells and not macrophages.
Option C: Squamocolumnar Metaplasia - this is incorrect as bacterial vaginosis is not associated with metaplasia.
Option D: Trichomonas - this is incorrect as bacterial vaginosis is caused by Gardnerella vaginalis and not Trichomonas. Trichomonas is seen as a motile protozoan in vaginal wet mount.

stem: Which among the following is anti-apoptotic in nature?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Anti-apoptotic proteins prolong the life of a cell by preventing apoptosis.
• Examples of anti-apoptotic proteins are:
• 1. BCL-1
• 2. BCL-XL
• 3. MCL-1
• Note: BCL-2XS is pro-apoptotic in nature.
• Pro-apoptotic molecules are:
• 1. BAK
• 2. BAX
• Sensors of stress for apoptosis are:
• 1. BIM
• 2. BID
• 3. BAD
• 4. PUMA
• 5. NOXA

Other Options:
Option A: BAX is a pro-apoptotic molecule. It is not an anti-apoptotic molecule.
Option B: PUMA is a sensor of stress for apoptosis. It is not an anti-apoptotic molecule.
Option C: BAK is a pro-apoptotic molecule. It is not an anti-apoptotic molecule.

stem: All the following statements are correct about BRCA-1, except
options: Involved in double strand DNA repair
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• BRCA-1 is a tumour suppressor gene.
• It is present on chromosome 17.
• It encodes a 1863 AA nuclear phosphoprotein.
• It is involved in DNA repair mechanisms of double stranded breaks.
• BRCA-2 is located on chromosome 13.
• BRCA-1 gene mutation predisposes an individual to the following types of cancers:
1. Breast
2. Ovarian
3. Prostate
4. Pancreas
• BRCA-2 mutation is associated with Fanconi anaemia, and not BRCA-1.

Other Options:
Option A: BRCA-1 is involved in double strand DNA repair. This is a true statement.
Option B: BRCA-1 is a tumour suppressor gene located on chromosome 17. This is a true statement.
Option C: BRCA mutation is involved in breast, ovarian, testicular and pancreatic cancers. Hence this is a true statement.

stem: An elderly patient was admitted with lymphadenopathy. The total leucocyte counts were found to be 68,000 per cu. mm. The histopathology revealed smudge cells. What would be the next line of management in this patient?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This is a case of chronic lymphoid leukaemia.
• The clinical pointers towards this diagnosis are:
1. Elderly age group
2. Increased leukocyte counts
3. Presence of mature leukocytes
4. Histopathology showing smudge cells
• The next line of management after diagnosing all the types of leukemias is flow cytometry for determining the CD markers.
• An exception to this is chronic myeloid leukaemia, where the translocation (9;22) is investigated.
• The next line of management of lymphomas is lymph node or bone marrow biopsy.

Other Options:
Option A: Bone marrow biopsy is an invasive process. Since the diagnosis in this case can be made from flow cytometry, due to the presence of characteristic cell markers, bone marrow biopsy comes later in the line of management.
Option C: PET scan stands for positron emission tomography. It is the investigation of choice for the diagnosis of metastasis. It is based on the warburg effect.
Option D: Cytogenetics involves testing samples of tissue, blood, or bone marrow in a laboratory to look for chromosomal anomalies or rearrangements. It is the next line of management in cases of chronic myeloid leukaemia.

stem: From the image depicted below, identify the type of ANA pattern
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Centromeric pattern of antinuclear antibodies (ANA) is characterised by the presence of multiple dotted areas of immunofluorescence representative of the centromeres in the genetic material.


• Diffuse pattern of antinuclear antibodies is characterised by presence of homogenous immunofluorescence.


• Speckled pattern of antinuclear antibodies is characterised by the presence of homogenous immunofluorescence with clear areas of non-fluorescence in between.


• Nucleolar pattern of antinuclear antibodies is characterised by the presence of 2-3 areas of immunofluorescence representing the nucleoli.


Other Options:
Option B: Diffuse pattern of antinuclear antibodies is characterised by presence of homogenous immunofluorescence. (Figure 2 above)
Option C: Speckled pattern of antinuclear antibodies is characterised by the presence of homogenous immunofluorescence with clear areas of non-fluorescence in between. (Figure 3 above)
Option D: Nucleolar pattern of antinuclear antibodies is characterised by the presence of 2-3 areas of immunofluorescence representing the nucleoli. (Figure 4 above)

stem:
Match the following disorders with the correct genetic abnormalities
List 1List 2
1. Trisomy 18a) Sickle cell disease
2. Point mutation of Glu to Val in Hbb) Patau syndrome
3. Trisomy 13c) Huntington disease
4. Trinucleotide repeat sequencesd) Edward syndrome
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Trisomy 18 is seen in Edward syndrome.
• It is characterised by low set ears, overlapping of fingers, heart and lung abnormalities, decreased muscle tone and club foot.
• Sickle cell anaemia shows point mutation of Glu to Val in the 6th position of the beta globin chain of haemoglobin.
• In conditions of hypoxia, the RBCs containing such haemoglobin undergo sickling leading to vaso-occlusive crisis.
• Trisomy 13 is seen in Patau syndrome.
• It is characterised by a small head, low set ears, cleft lip and cleft palate.
• Huntington's disease is characterised by trinucleotide repeat extension of CAG.
• It is characterised by increased muscle spasm, involuntary movements (chorea), difficulty in speech and cognitive and psychiatric symptoms.

Other Options:
Options A, C and D: These are incorrect matches of the given conditions.

stem: From the pedigree chart depicted below, the pattern of inheritance would be
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In the given pedigree chart, males and females are equally affected.
• This points towards an autosomal mode of inheritance.
• In sex linked inheritance, a particular sex predilection exists. Ex: X linked recessive traits affect males much more than females.
• In the given pedigree, there is no skip generation.
• This points to the dominant nature of inheritance i.e. autosomal dominant.
• Thus, the variant is present even in one allele of a gene. It will get manifested in autosomal dominant inheritance
• Ex: Huntington disease, Marfan syndrome.

Other Options:
Option B: Autosomal recessive disorders show skip generation in their pedigree and are manifested only when both the alleles of a gene are affected. Males and females are equally affected. Ex: Cystic fibrosis, Sickle cell anemia.
Option C: X linked recessive traits affect males much more than females.
Option D: In mitochondrial inheritance, a trait present in the maternal genes is passed on to none of the offspring. Ex: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON).

stem: All the following statements about amyloidosis are correct, except
options: Beta pleated sheet in X-ray crystallography
Amyloidosis named due to accumulation related to starch
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• AL is a primary type of amyloidosis.
• The source of this type of amyloid proteins is the light chain of immunoglobulins produced by the plasma cells in multiple myeloma.
• It is a systemic type of amyloidosis.
• It affects the kidneys, heart, liver, gastrointestinal system and nervous system.
• Examples of secondary amyloidosis include:
1. AA (amyloid associated)
2. ATTR (familial amyloidosis)
3. Acal (in medullary carcinoma of thyroid)

Other Options:
Option A: Amyloidosis refers to an extracellular deposition of fibrillation proteinaceous material. This is a true statement.
Option B: X ray crystallography of amyloid proteins reveal beta pleated sheaths. This is a true statement.
Option D: Amyloid was initially named so due to its resemblance with the structure of starch. However, it is a protein. Thus, this is a true statement.

stem: Identify the cell depicted in the image given below
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• This is an electron microscopic view of a hairy cell. The small projections on the cell membrane are suggestive of this.
• Hairy cells are seen in hairy cell leukaemia.
• They exhibit a characteristic immunophenotypic profile that is strongly positive for pan-B-cell markers, positive for CD103, CD11c and CD25 and usually negative for CD5, CD10, and CD23.

Other Options:
Option A: Sickle cells are seen as concavo-convex cells (sickle shaped) on electron microscopy. They are seen in sickle cell anaemia.
Option B: Target cells are bell shaped cells on electron microscopy. They are seen in certain types of anaemia like thalassemia and post splenectomy.
Option D: Gaucher's cells show crumpled tissue paper appearance on electron microscopy. They are seen in Gaucher's disease, a type of sphingolipidoses.

stem: All the following can be associated with massive blood transfusion, except
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Massive blood transfusion can be defined as replacement of more than one time of the blood volume in 24 hours or >50% of blood volume in 4 hours.
• Due to massive blood transfusion, there occurs lysis of red blood cells.
• This lysis results in hyperkalemia and not hypokalemia.
• The following are the effects of massive blood transfusion:
1. Hypothermia
2. Hyperkalemia
3. Citrate toxicity
4. Hypocalcemia
5. Fluid overload
6. Depletion of platelets and coagulation factors
7. Hypomagnesemia

Other Options:
Option A: Hypothermia can occur due to transfusion of blood whose temperature is at a lower level than normal body temperature. It is thus a complication of massive blood transfusion.
Option C: Hypocalcemia is a consequence of massive blood transfusion. This is a correct statement.
Option D: Hypomagnesemia is a consequence of massive blood transfusion. This is a correct statement.

stem: In a patient with alcoholic liver disease, which of the following can be the specific marker?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• GGT stands for gamma glutamyl transferase.
• It is a specific marker for alcoholic liver disease.
• It is also raised in an obstructive type of jaundice.
• The main role of GGT in the liver is the extracellular catabolism of glutathione, a major antioxidant and cellular defence mechanism. It is an important enzyme in the gamma glutamyl cycle.
• Thus, the increase in GGT decreased glutathione, thereby predisposing an individual to higher levels of free radicals and potential complications like atherosclerosis.
• Obstructive jaundice show a rise in the following markers:
1. ALP (alkaline phosphatase)
2. 5' nucleotidase
3. Gamma glutamyl transferase

Other Options:
Option B: ALT stands for alanine transferase. It is raised in hepatocellular jaundice.
Option C: Bilirubin is the excretory form of cholesterol in the body. It is a bile pigment. It can be of two types, conjugated and unconjugated.
Option D: AST stands for aspartate transferase. It is raised in hepatocellular jaundice.

stem: Which of the following genes is responsible for the regulation of T lymphocytes by the thymus?
subject: Pathology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
The AIRE gene is responsible for regulation of T lymphocytes in the thymus.
• AIRE stands for autoimmune regulator gene.
• The AIRE gene is a transcription regulator of the protein called the autoimmune regulator. This protein is active primarily in the thymus
• Malfunctioning of this gene has been implicated in a generalised autoimmune disease called autoimmune polyendocrinopathy candidiasis ectodermal dystrophy (APECED).

Other Options:
Option A: RAG 1 (recombination activating gene 1) is a highly conserved gene in humans. It provides instructions for making a member of a group of proteins called the RAG complex. This complex is active in B cells and T cells.
Option C: NOTCH 3 encodes for Neurogenic locus notch homolog protein 3. Its mutation causes a disease named CADASIL (cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy).
Option D: RAG 2 (recombination activating gene 2) is a highly conserved gene in humans. It is located on chromosome 11 and encodes a lymphocyte specific protein.

stem: A patient with renal failure has undergone hemodialysis. Which of the following amyloid proteins would be expected to get deposited in this patient?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In a patient undergoing dialysis, A Beta 2m type of amyloid will be deposited.
• This is because A beta 2 microglobulin is unable to pass through the dialysing membrane.
• Thereby its concentration increases in the human body.
• It generally affects the bone and joints.
• Symptoms include carpal tunnel syndrome, tendinitis and shoulder pain.
• It may also cause severe pulmonary hypertension and heart failure.

Other Options:
Option A: AL refers to light chain amyloid. It is a type of primary amyloidosis where light chains of Immunoglobulins are deposited in multiple myeloma.
Option B: ATTR is a familial type of amyloidosis. It refers to amyloid transthyretin. Transthyretin is a transport protein for thyroid hormones and retinol in the body.
Option D: A cal refers to amyloid calcitonin. It is deposited in medullary carcinoma of thyroid, where the concentrations of calcitonin are increased.

stem: Which among the following disorders is associated with the absence of CD40 ligand?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The normal action of CD40 ligand is class switching.
• This enables formation of different types of immunoglobulins.
• Absence of CD40 leads to defective class switching.
• Thus, only IgM is formed in large quantities.
• The rest of immunoglobulins are deficient.
• These patients present with gastrointestinal features like malabsorption and respiratory infections with increased levels of IgM, but decreased levels of other types of immunoglobulins.

Other Options:
Option A: Agammaglobulinemia is the absence of gamma globulins. It is not seen with CD40 absence.
Option C: SCID stands for Severe combined Immunodeficiency Disease. It is due to defective adenosine deaminase.
Option D: IgA nephropathy is a renal condition due to accumulation of IgA over glomerulus (also known as Berger's disease).

stem: From the following statements about the blood transfusion products, identify the correct statement
options: Fresh frozen plasma can be stored at -30 degree C for 2 years
Cryoprecipitate can be stored at 2-6 degree C
Platelets can be stored at 24 degree C for 5 days
Packed red blood cells can be stored at -10 degree C for 3 weeks
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Platelets can be stored at 24 degree C for 5 days. This statement is true.
• Platelets can be stored at room temperature (20-24 degree C).
• They have a shelf life of approximately 5 days.
• Cryoprecipitate is kept at freezing temperatures (lower than -18 degree C).
• Fresh frozen plasma has a shelf life of one year.
• Packed red blood cells are kept at 2-6 degrees celsius.
• Thus, option C is correct.

Other Options:
Option A: Fresh frozen plasma can be stored at -30 degree C for 2 years - this is false as it can be stored for one year.
Option B: Cryoprecipitate can be stored at 2-6 degree C - this is false. Cryoprecipitate is kept at freezing temperatures (lower than -18 degree C).
Option D: Packed red blood cells can be stored at -10 degree C for 3 weeks - this is false as packed RBC is kept at 2-6 degrees celsius.

stem: All the following are altered DNA sequence detection techniques, except
subject: Pathology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Flow cytometry is a test which detects antigens on a cell.
• It is not used for gene sequencing, as it does not deal with genetic material.
• Instead it deals with the antigens on the cell surface and this can be used for the diagnosis of various diseases.
• For example, it can detect absence of CD40 ligand on cells, which is diagnostic for Hyper IgM syndrome.

Other Options:
Option A: Pyrosequencing is a replication based technique of gene sequencing based on light detection (released pyrophosphate).
Option C: RFLP stands for restriction fragment length polymorphism. It is a technique of gene sequencing, which can be used in forensics (paternity tests).
Option D: Sanger sequencing, also known as chain termination sequencing, was the first technique of gene sequencing.
