stem: Which of the following is the most common cause for hypercoagulable state?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Factor 5 leiden mutation is the most common cause for hypercoagulable state.
• Normally, Factor 5 leiden allows Factor Xa to activate prothrombin into thrombin.
• The mutation causes overactivation of this pathway, leading to hypercoagulable state.
• Even though the condition is not very common, it is the most common cause of hypercoagulable state.

Other Options:
Option A: Protein C deficiency - It is an extremely rare condition and can lead to hypercoagulable state. Protein C is involved in the regulation of the factors VIIa and Va.
Option C: Prothrombin mutation - It is a rare condition compared to factor 5 leiden mutation. Prothrombin (coagulation factor II) is a vitamin K dependent clotting factor.
Option D: Antithrombin III mutation - It is a rare condition compared to factor 5 leiden mutation. Antithrombin III is involved in the inactivation of the clotting factors including thrombin.

stem:
Match the following disorders with the correct findings
DisorderFinding

2. Caplan syndromeb) Crazy pavement pattern
3. Silicosisc) Lower lung lobe involved
4. Mesotheliomad) Coal worker pneumoconiosis
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Caplan syndrome is another name for coal workers pneumoconiosis along with rheumatoid arthritis.
• Crazy pavement pattern is seen in silicosis, which is due to inhalation of silica dust.
• Mesothelioma is a tumour of the pleural membranes. It can be associated with pleural effusion.
• Asbestosis is a pneumoconiosis due to inhalation of asbestos dust and it commonly involves the lower lobe of lung.

Other Options:

stem:
Find the Kappa statistics.
IGRA Suspect TBNot TBTotal
TST suspect TB8040120
No TB206080
Total100100200
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Kappa statistic is done to check the inter observer agreement
• Here, out of 200 people, two tests are done - Tuberculin test and IGRA
• Kappa statistic can be calculated by the following formula:
• (Percent agreement observed - Percentage agreement expected by chance) / (1 - Percent agreement expected by chance)
• Percent agreement observed = (80 + 60) / 200 = 0.7
• Percent agreement expected by chance:
• Probability of both tests randomly saying YES = 100/200 x 120/200 = 0.5 x 0.6 = 0.3
• Probability of both tests randomly saying NO = 100/200 x 80/120 = 0.5 x 0.4 = 0.2
• Percent agreement expected by chance = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5
• Thus, applying these values into the equation, Kappa statistic = 0.7 - 0.5 / 1 - 0.5 = 0.2 / 0.5 = 0.4

Other Options:
Options A, B and D: These options are not the correct values

stem: Which of the following is not the guideline for studies?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• For various studies, there are guidelines on how to report these studies.
• These can be summarised as follows :
1. Randomised Controlled Trials (RCT) - CONSORT
2. Systematic review and meta analysis - PRISMA
3. Observational studies - STROBE
4. Case Reports - CARE
5. Meta analysis of observational studies - MOOSE
6. Diagnostic accuracy studies - STARD
• As we see, option A is incorrect as CARE is the guideline for case reports.

Other Options:
Option B: CONSORT : RCT – correct match.
Option C: PRISMA : Systematic Review & meta analysis – correct match.
Option D: MOOSE : Observational – correct match.

stem: Which of the following has the correct incubation period?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Syphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum and is transmitted by sexual contact.
• It causes painless genital ulcers without much bleeding.
• The incubation period of syphilis is 10-90 days.
• Following this period, it develops primary syphilis characterised by painless ulcers (chancres) in the genital region
• If untreated, the primary syphilis can progress to secondary or even tertiary syphilis.

Other Options:
Option B: Chicken Pox 5 days - wrong, as it has an incubation period of 14 to 16 days.
Option C: COVID 1-2 days - wrong, as it has an incubation period of 2-14 days.
Option D: Hep A 1-3 days - wrong, as it has an incubation period of 15 to 50 days.

stem: The given mathematical formula is used for calculation of
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• This formula is for the calculation of standard deviation (SD).
• Standard deviation is a quantity which expresses how much the members of a group differ from the mean value for the group.
• It is a measure of how dispersed the data is in relation to the mean.
• It is calculated as the root mean square value of individual deviations from the mean.


Other Options:
Option B: Variance is the square of standard deviation.
Option C: Confidence intervals measure the degree of uncertainty or certainty in a sampling method.
Option D: Mean is the average of the values in a data set.

stem: Which of the following is not useful to determine normalcy of data?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Normality of data means, we want to check if the data is normally distributed or is it skewed.
• The following are used to determine normalcy of data:
1. Box and whisker plot
2. Histogram
3. Stem and leaf plot
4. P-P plot
5. Kolmogorov- smirnov test
6. Shapiro wilk test
• As we can see, Mantel score is not used to determine normalcy of data.
• Mantel-Haenszel method is associated with pooled odds ratio of stratified data.
• If data is of normal distribution, we can use parametric tests.

Other Options:
Option A, B and C: Box and Whisker plot, Histogram and Shapiro wilk test are used to determine the normalcy of data as mentioned above.

stem: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: Mode is for the qualitative data
Mean is affected by extreme values
Mode is least affected by extreme values
Median absolute deviation (MAD) is not a measure of the variability of a univariate sample of quantitative data.
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Median absolute deviation (MAD) is a measure of variability of a univariate sample of quantitative data.
• It is correct as mean can be influenced by outliers with extreme values.
• Mode is for the qualitative data. This is correct as mode can be used for both qualitative and quantitative data.
• Mode is least affected by extreme values. This is correct as mode is just measuring frequency of occurrence of a value.

Other Options:
Option A: Mode is for both qualitative and quantitative data.
Option B: Mean can be influenced by outliers with extreme values.
Option C: Mode is least affected by extreme values, as mode is just measuring frequency of occurrence of a value.

stem: A man is on immunosuppressants bitten by a dog (both man and dog immunized), simple skin lesion is there. What is the next step of management?
subject: Medicine, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• The newer guidelines suggest that prophylactic antibiotic can be given in a case of dog bite and this is applicable only when the patient is immunocompromised, diabetics or old people.
• For example, patient on immunosuppressants, a broad spectrum antibiotic like Amoxyclav (amoxicillin/clavulanate) is preferred.

Other Options:
Option A: Observation - mere observation is no longer recommended for a case of dog bite.
Option C: Metronidazole has no efficacy against common organisms implicated in dog bite.
Option D: Ciprofloxacin is not a broad spectrum antibiotic, hence is not preferred as a prophylactic antibiotic.

stem: In Kaplan Meier Survival curve/plot, bands used for confidence intervals are
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Kaplan-Meier Survival curve/plot is used for calculating the survival rate of diseases.
• For example, the 5 year survival rate of cancers.
• Life table analysis also performs the same function.
• Hall-Wellner bands are used for the confidence intervals in this curve.
• Confidence intervals measure the degree of uncertainty or certainty in a sampling method.
• The following diagram shows the Kaplan Meier Survival curve.

Other Options:
Option B, C and D: Prediction bands, Brookmeyer and Crowley and Point band are not used for confidence intervals in Kaplan-Meier survival plot.

stem: How much population falls between median and median ± 1SD in a case of normal distribution?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• In a normal distribution curve, the population between median ± 1SD is 68% of the total.
• Similarly, the population between median ± 2SD is 95% of the total and the population between median ± 3SD is 99% of the total
• Thus, according to the question, it is 68% or 0.68.
• Standard deviation is a quantity which expresses how much the members of a group differ from the mean value for the group. It is a measure of how dispersed the data is in relation to the mean.
• The median is the value in a dataset which lies in the middle of all values, when they are arranged in ascending or descending order.
• The mode is the value which occurs with highest frequency in the dataset.

Other Options:
Option A, C and D: The other options are Incorrect values.

stem: 30K large cohort is followed up for 10 years. 50 cases develop and rest controls have been taken. What kind of study design is it?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• A cohort study is a prospective study in which a group of individuals are selected and the data collection is carried into the future.
• This study is a cohort study, within which the investigator is assigning cases and controls.
• Case-control study is a retrospective study, where cases and controls are decided.
• Thus, it falls in the category of nested case control study.
• A nested case control study is a cohort study in which case control studies are performed within a cohort, over a long period of time.
• Thus, option A is the correct answer.

Other Options:
Option B: Retrospective Cohort is one in which the cohort is selected and the investigator goes back in time to find out the documented data for the cohort.
Option C: In cross sectional study, the data collection is done at a specific point of time.
Option D: Meta Analysis is a study which summarises the findings of many other studies.

stem: Live Vaccination is contraindicated in all, except
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Live vaccines are not contraindicated in complement deficiency.
• The complement deficiency is a primary Immunodeficiency disorder associated with absent or defective complement proteins.
• It has been shown that live vaccine does not cause increased risk of infection in patients with complement deficiency.
• Live vaccines are contraindicated in immunocompromised patients like:
1. Wiskott Aldrich syndrome
2. Di-george syndrome
3. Ataxia telangiectasia

Other Options:
Option A: Wiskott Aldrich syndrome is due to defect in the WASP gene leading to thrombocytopenia, eczema and recurrent infections.
Option B: Di-george syndrome is due to deletion of the long arm of chromosome 22, which is associated with hypoplasia or aplasia of thymus. Live vaccines are contraindicated.
Option C: Ataxia telangiectasia is a DNA repair defect leading to cerebellar dysfunction and telangiectasia over skin.

stem: Vector given in the image transmits?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows the Trombiculid mite.
• It transmits Scrub typhus and Rickettsial pox.
• Thus, option A is correct.
• Scrub typhus is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi from the rickettsial group of organisms.
• It is associated with buboes, eschars over skin and fever.
• The control of mites is achieved by eliminating the hosts (rodents or fowl), their nests and chemically controlling existing mite populations.

Other Options:
Option B: Crimean-congo haemorrhagic fever has Ixodid tick as its vector.
Option C: Chagas disease has triatomine bug as its vector.
Option D: Onchocerciasis has blackfly as its vector.

stem: Which of the following statistical charts is best used to evaluate the normal distribution of data?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Histogram is the best statistical chart to evaluate normal distribution of data.
• Q-Q plot and Kolmogorov - smirnov test are also used to determine normalcy of data.

stem: Which of the following is not a part of the RIGHT slogan for snake bite treatment?
subject: FMT, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The RIGHT slogan includes:
• Reassurance: This helps allay anxiety as stress can be a cause of deaths in patients of snake bite.
• Immobilisation: Immobilisation of the limb which has been bitten prevents the venom from spreading to other parts of the body
• Get to Hospital: Enables early diagnosis and treatment.
• Tell the doctor about emergence of symptoms.
• Incision is not a part of RIGHT slogan and is actually contraindicated in a case of snake bite.

Other Options:
Option A: Reassurance is a part of RIGHT slogan.
Option C: Get to hospital is a part of RIGHT slogan.
Option D: Immobilisation is a part of RIGHT slogan.

stem: Which of the following is false in respect to autopsy findings?
options: Adipocere occurs after the body comes out of water
Cadaveric Spasms occur in specific muscles
Post-mortem lividity is seen in lower limbs due to drowning
Greenish colour over right iliac fossa is an early sign of putrefaction
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Post-mortem lividity is not seen in lower limbs in death by drowning.
• It is seen in dependent portions of the body.
• In a case of drowning, the body is in face down - buttock up position.
• Thus, post mortem lividity is seen in head, tips of upper and lower limbs and the knees (depending on the position of the body)

Other Options:
Option A: Adipocere occurs after the body comes out of water. This is a true statement. Water content in tissue is required for adipocere formation
Option B: Cadaveric spasms occur in specific muscles. This is a true statement. It is seen after emotionally charged deaths in specific muscles.
Option D: Greenish colour over right iliac fossa is an early sign of putrefaction. This is a true statement. It is due to bacterial decomposition and formation of sulfhaem.

stem: A person has died from drowning. All are probable signs of antemortem drowning, except
options: White froth coming from mouth and nostrils
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• 'Washerwoman hands' refers to wrinkling of skin due to prolonged exposure to water.
• It is seen when skin is in contact with water for long duration of time.
• The fingers become pale and show soddening, bleaching and wrinkling.
• It can be seen in both antemortem and postmortem drowning, but commonly seen in cases of postmortem drowning.

Other Options:
Option A: Emphysema aquosum is a sign of antemortem drowning.
Option C: Diatoms test positive. Diatoms are siliceous organisms which are found in soil. They get inhaled while drowning and go into the bone marrow via blood.
Option D: White froth coming from mouth and nostrils is a classical sign of antemortem drowning.

stem: Autopsy image showing extensive charring and red line of demarcation?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Charring and red line of demarcation (as shown in the image) are the classical signs for antemortem burns.
• The antemortem burns are associated with vesicles or blisters with serous fluid and there can be signs of healing with granulation tissue.
• In postmortem burns, there will be no line of redness.
• The other options may cause blisters, but they do not cause charring of skin and red line of demarcation.

Other Options:
Option B: Barbiturate poisoning doesn't cause charring. It can cause drowsiness, respiratory depression and drug automatism.
Option C: Edema due to chemical burn doesn't cause charring. It causes skin blisters and erythematous rashes.
Option D: Pemphigus vulgaris doesn't cause charring. It is a bullous disorder characterised by blistering of the skin.

stem: Black foot disease is associated with which of the following poisoning?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Arsenic poisoning can lead to necrosis and gangrene of limbs.
• This can lead to blackening of lower limb.
• This appearance is called black foot disease.
• Acute arsenic poisoning can either involve the gastrointestinal tract (GIT) or the central nervous system (CNS).
• Chronic poisoning presents with raindrop pigmentation of skin or black foot disease.
• The other options do not cause black foot disease.

Other Options:
Option A: Cadmium leads to Itai-Itai disease causing pathological fractures.
Option C: Antimony leads to pneumoconiosis in the lungs.
Option D: Bismuth leads to myoclonic encephalopathy.

stem: A 16 year old girl was brought to you by police with her mother for alleged case of rape. What will be the first step done by you as a medical officer?
options: Start the examination and document the injury
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• In a case of sexual assault, consent is the foremost step in examination.
• Examination of victim of sexual assault comes under 164A CrPC.
• If the victim is less than 12 years, the consent of her guardians is taken.
• If the victim refuses to give consent, an informed refusal form is submitted.

Other Options:
Option A: Start the examination and document the injury - this step needs to be done only after consent is taken.
Option B: Take proper detailed history - this step needs to be done only after consent is taken.
Option C: Preserve clothing - this step needs to be done only after consent is taken.

stem: A person with firearm injury only an abrasion collar seen. Identify the range?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• The injury shows only an abrasion collar.
• It does not show blackening, tattooing, burning or singeing.
• It is likely to be a distant shot.
• An abrasion collar is due to the effect of the bullet on the periphery of the wound.
• It is accompanied by a grease collar.
• The image below shows an abrasion collar.


Other Options:
Option A: Close shot shows blackening due to smoke.
Option B: Near shot shows blackening and tattooing.
Option C: Contact shot shows muzzle imprint.

stem:
Which of the following is a direct Gonioscope?
1.Goldman's
2. Koeppe's
3. Zeiss
4. Richardson
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Gonioscopy is for the evaluation of the angle of the anterior chamber
• It is of two types: direct and indirect
• In indirect gonioscopy, the light rays will be reflected by using a mirror in the contact lens (mirrored goniolens), whereas in direct gonioscopy, no mirror will be used (goniolens)
• Indirect gonioscopes are of two types:
1. Goldmann
2. Zeiss
3. Koeppe's
4. Richardson
5. Direct gonioscopes can be:

Other Options:
Option A: 2 only - 4 is also correct.
Option C: 1,2,3 - 4 and 2 are incorrect.
Option D: 1,5,4 - 1 is incorrect.

stem: What is the sequence of staining in case of dry eye?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Fluorescein stains the area where epithelium is absent.
• It can be used for evaluation of dry eye and corneal ulcers.
• Lissamine green is mainly used for staining devitalized cells.
• Rose Bengal is used for staining devitalized cells.
• The order of staining is:
1. Fluorescein
2. Lissamine green
3. Rose Bengal

Other Options:
Option A, B and C: The other options are incorrect about the order (sequence) of staining with the dyes.

stem: The following are the images from a patient before and after the use of phenylephrine eye drops. The probable diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Phenylephrine drops blanch superficial blood vessels, leading to pale appearance, as shown in the pictures.
• This means that superficial vessel congestion is the pathology in the question, which leads to diagnosis of episcleritis.
• Episcleritis is the congestion of superficial blood vessels, while scleritis is the congestion of deep vessels.

Other Options:
Option B: Scleritis is the congestion of deep vessels too and phenylephrine wouldn't be able to blanch it fully.
Option C: Phlycten is a nodule on the conjunctiva due to type 4 hypersensitivity to a particular antigen.
Option D: Tenon cysts are conjunctival cysts and are unrelated to the pathology shown in the image.

stem: Choose the structure marked
subject: Ophthalmology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The area shown is between the post equatorial zonules and the anterior face of the hyaloid.
• This place is called as the Canal of Petit.
• It provides support to lens, helps in accommodation and provides a structural framework for movement of aqueous and vitreous.
• Canal of Hanover is the area between pre and post equatorial zonules.


Other Options:
Option A: Egger's line is the circular line of adhesion between the vitreous and posterior lens.
Option B: Lens capsules is covering the cortex of the lens.
Option C: Canal of Hanover is the area between pre and post equatorial zonules.

stem: Which of the following is true about glaucoma?
options: Early and specific feature is isopter contraction and baring of blind spot
In a static perimeter, intensity of stimulus is kept constant while it is moved
VFD to be associated with glaucoma only if it is associated with ONH changes
Kinetic perimeter is best for monitoring VF changes in a glaucoma patient
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Glaucoma is a chronic progressive optic neuropathy most commonly associated with Increase in Intraocular pressure (IOP).
• It is associated with visual field defects (VFD), raised IOP and optic disc changes.
• Early features of VF changes are Isopter contraction and baring of blind spots. However they are not specific for glaucoma.
• VFD to be associated with glaucoma only if it is associated with optic nerve head changes.
• This is true because presence of at least two out of three entities is required for diagnosis of glaucoma.

Other Options:
Option A: Early and specific feature is Isopter contraction and baring of blind spots - this is false as these are not specific of glaucoma.
Option B: In a static perimeter, intensity of stimulus is kept constant while it is moved - this is false as the Intensity changes as it is moved.
Option D: Kinetic perimeter is best for monitoring VF changes in a glaucoma patient - this is false as kinetic perimetry is not the best for monitoring VF changes.

stem: Name the nerve block in this procedure
subject: Ophthalmology, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• The nasociliary nerve is part of the infratrochlear nerve, which is a branch of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (Cranial nerve V1).
• The injection is typically administered near the medial canthus (inner corner of the eye) or just below the eye.
• It is a type of regional anaesthesia for pre-operative preparation of the patient.
• Nasociliary nerve blocks are commonly used in ophthalmic surgeries, such as cataract surgery or certain types of corneal procedures.

Other Options:
Option B: Sphenopalatine block is given nasally.
Option C: Anterior ethmoid block is given on the dorsum of the nose.
Option D: Vidian nerve block is given nasally.

stem:
Match the following with their regards to thyroid swelling obstruction:
StructureSymptom
A. Trachea1. Dysphasia
B. Oesophagus2. Hoarseness of voice
C. Recurrent laryngeal nerve3. Horner's syndrome
D. Sympathetic chain4. Dyspnoea
subject: Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Oesophagus compression causes dysphasia (difficulty in swallowing).
• Tracheal compression causes dyspnea (difficulty in breathing).
• Recurrent laryngeal nerve compression causes hoarseness of voice.
• Sympathetic chain compression causes Horner's syndrome.

The characteristic features of Horner's syndrome include:
• 1. Ptosis: Drooping of the upper eyelid on the affected side.
• 2. Miosis: Constriction of the pupil on the affected side, leading to a smaller pupil size.
• 3. Anhidrosis: Decreased or absent sweating on the affected side of the face.
• 4. Enophthalmos: Sinking of the eyeball within the eye socket, resulting in a sunken appearance of the eye.
• 5. Absent ciliospinal reflex: Absence of a normal pupillary dilation response, when the back of the neck is stimulated.

• Thus, the correct option is Option C.

Other Options:
Options A, B and D: The other options are incorrect matches.

stem:
Arrange in order of BERA
1. Spiral ganglions
2. Cochlear nucleus
3. Sup. Olivary nucleus
4. Lateral lemniscus
5. Inferior nucleus
6. Medial geniculate
7. Auditory cortex
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Brainstem evoked response audiometry (BERA) is a test used in the evaluation of hearing threshold.
• It is done for infants and younger patients and it is an objective test (does not require patient cooperation).
• The signal goes in the following order:
1. Spiral ganglion
2. Cochlear nucleus
3. Superior olivary nucleus
4. Lateral lemniscus
5. Inferior colliculus
6. Medial geniculate body
7. Auditory cortex
• Thus, the correct option is Option A.

Other Options:
Option B, C and D: The other options are Incorrect sequences.

stem: An elderly male comes with a polyp hanging from the nose. He takes a bath in the pond regularly. What is the diagnosis?
subject: ENT, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows strawberry-like lesion in the nose and is likely to be Rhinosporidiosis.
• It is a chronic granulomatous condition of the mucous membrane (commonly the nasal cavity) caused by Rhinosporidium seeberi, an aquatic protist.
• It is associated with taking baths in stagnant water.
• These patients present with nasal obstruction (unilateral), epistaxis, local pruritus and postnasal discharge.
• Treatment involves local excision of the lesion.

Other Options:
Option A: Rhinoscleroma is caused by Klebsiella rhinoscleromatis and is associated with atrophic phase followed by sclerotic phase.
Option C: Pemphigus vulgaris is associated with bullae on the skin.
Option D: Nasal polyp wouldn't be associated with the history of taking baths at ponds. It has a pale, fleshy appearance.

stem: What is the likely diagnosis of the image given below?
subject: ENT, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Ranula is a large cyst filled with fluid that arises in the floor of the oral cavity.
• It may result from a blocked salivary gland.
• They present as a bluish translucent swelling that may elevate the tongue.
• If the lesion is symptomatic, surgical excision of the lump as well as the sublingual salivary gland may be done.

Other Options:
Option B: Mucocele is a benign mucous containing cystic lesion of minor salivary glands.
Option C: Ludwig angina involves the base of the oral cavity giving rise to a woody, brawny appearance of the floor of the mouth.
Option D: In dermoid cyst, there will be entrapment of skin-associated structures during foetal development (benign developmental cysts).

stem: A 14 year old female patient presents to the ENT OPD with complaints that she can understand the language and hear the sound, but unable to understand the meaning. Her Pure tone audiometry and ABR is normal. What is the likely diagnosis in this patient?
subject: ENT, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The clinical stem points to difficulty in comprehension, which suggests a lesion in the comprehension centre (sensory speech area) of the brain, the Wernicke's area.
• The Wernicke's centre is responsible for comprehension of heard language and is located in the superior temporal gyrus of the dominant hemisphere (Area 22).
• Lesion in this area causes Wernicke's aphasia leading to difficulty in comprehension.
• Hearing is normal as demonstrated by pure tone audiometry.

Other Options:
Option A: Malingering is the phenomenon, where a patient voluntarily feigns an illness for an ulterior motive.
Option C: In Otosclerosis, there will be fibrous replacement of bony architecture of the stapes, leading to fixation of stapes footplate.
Option D: Michael aplasia is also called the complete labyrinthine aplasia. The complete inner ear is not formed, leading to hearing and balance difficulties.

stem: Which of the following is incorrect about QPR?
options: Depth of chest compressions in adult is 5 to 6 cm
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) begins with checking the scene safety and calling for help.
• Then, check the breath and Pulse of the patient.
• If breathing and Pulse are absent, start CPR.
• It consists of 5 cycles of chest compressions and breaths in the ratio 30:2.
• If rhythm is non shockable, continue CPR.
1. Push hard (5-6 cm depth)
2. Push fast (100-120 /min)
3. Minimize interruptions between compressions
4. Allow adequate chest recoil
5. Change compressor every 2 minutes
6. No excessive ventilation

Other Options:
Option A: Rate of compression is 100-120/min - this is true.
Option C: Depth of chest compressions in adults is 5 to 6 cm - this is true as 5-6 cm depth of compression ensures the ratio 30:2.
Option D: Allow adequate chest recoil - this is true as chest recoil allows blood to be pumped, which provides perfusion to organs.

stem: Acute pancreatitis patient, Intubated on ventilator with following settings - TV- 420ml, RR-32 bpm, PEEP-12cm, FiO2- 0.9. On ABG, pH-7.33, pCO2- 48, pO2-110, HCO3- 28, SPO2-100%. What is the next step in management?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In this case, the patient has high pO2.
• Decrease In FiO2 will result in decreased pO2 and spO2, without altering pCO2 and pH.
• Thus, it is the most preferred option.
• Decrease in respiratory rate results in decreased pO2 and spO2, but it increases pCO2. Thus it is not preferred.
• Increasing tidal volume will not benefit the patient, as the tidal volume is already good for this patient.
• Increasing PEEP will not benefit the pO2 status of this patient.

Other Options:
Option B: Decrease in respiratory rate results in decreased pO2 and spO2, but it increases pCO2.
Option C: Increasing tidal volume will not benefit the patient, as the tidal volume is already good for this patient.
Option D: Increasing PEEP will not benefit the pO2 status of this patient.

stem:
A 15 year old boy reported to the emergency department with breathing difficulty. Monitor was attached and the following graph was seen on capnography. What can be the diagnosis?
A. Acute bronchial asthma
B. Pericarditis
C. Cardiac tamponade
D. Bronchospasm
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The capnography curve shows the amount of CO2 exiting the body.
• In this curve, the CO2 is flowing in regular intervals.
• However, the curve shows an upturned slope, which is called as a shark fin appearance.
• A shark fin appearance is seen, where there is an airway obstruction.
• Airway obstruction is seen in acute bronchial asthma and bronchospasm.
• Thus a and d are correct.

Other Options:
Option A: a,b - Pericarditis is not associated with shark fin appearance.
Option B: b,c - Pericarditis and cardiac tamponade are not associated with shark fin appearance.
Option C: a,c - Cardiac tamponade is not associated with shark fin appearance.

stem: Which of the following tests is depicted in the following image?
subject: Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Allen test is done to check whether ulnar artery collaterals are patent.
• This test is done before a radial artery cannulation.
• Artery lines are used for beat to beat BP measurement for patients.
• It is used in acutely sick patients, who are in ICU and during major surgery.
• If artery is not patent and cannulation is done, it may lead to gangrene due to ischemia.
• If blanching disappears within 7 seconds, it is normal.

Other Options:
Option B: Durkan's test is used for carpal tunnel syndrome, by pressing on median nerve.
Option C: Ross test is done for identification of thoracic outlet syndrome.
Option D: Adson test is another provocative test for thoracic outlet syndrome.

stem:
Match the findings on wood lamp for following diseases
DiseasesAppearance
1. Erythrasmaa. Yellow
2. Pityriasis Versicolorb. Coral red
3. Tinea Capitisc. White
4. Vitiligod. Green
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Woods lamp is an instrument, which emits light on skin lesions and fluorescence can be observed on the lesions.
• The light is of wavelength 365 nm.
• The filter used contains barium Silicate and 9% nickel oxide.

The colour of fluorescence for lesions are given below:
• 1. Erythrasma - coral red
• 2. Pityriasis versicolor - yellow
• 3. Tinea capitis - green
• 4. Vitiligo - milky white

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: The other options are incorrect matches of the conditions.

stem: Non Scarring Alopecia is seen in all of the following, except
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Frontal fibrosing alopecia is a scarring type of alopecia.
• It is seen in perimenopausal aged females.
• The anterior hairline recedes backwards and there is wide forehead.
• Other causes of scarring alopecia are Lichen planopilaris, Kerlon and Discoid Lupus Erythematosus.
• Hint: Remember non scarring alopecia is seen with conditions starting with 'A' and 'T'.
• These include Androgenic alopecia, Alopecia areata, Trichotillomania & Telogen effluvium.

Other Options:
Option A: Androgenic alopecia is a non scarring alopecia seen in males & females in peculiar pattern.
Option C: Alopecia areata is a non scarring alopecia due to autoimmune process.
Option D: Telogen effluvium is a non scarring alopecia due to stress conditions.

stem: A patient on PDL-1 inhibitor presented with multiple blisters all over the body, on direct immunofluorescence, linear deposits of IgG/C3 were seen, What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• PDL-1 Inhibitors are Immune checkpoint Inhibitors used in certain cancers.
• They are associated with side effects like bullous pemphigoid.
• The image (Immunofluorescence) shows linear deposits of IgG and C3 in the basement membrane zone.
• This is seen with Bullous pemphigoid, which is an autoimmune blistering disorder.
• It causes large, tense blisters on the skin and mucous membranes.
• Treatment involves corticosteroids, Immunosuppressive medications and regular monitoring.

Other Options:
Option A: Pemphigus vulgaris shows fishnet pattern or chicken wire pattern of IgG/C3.
Option B: Dermatitis Herpetiformis shows granular IgA deposition over papillary tips.
Option D: Pemphigus Foliaceus shows IgG/C3 deposition in epidermis.

stem: A 24 year male presents to dermatology OPD with a scaly non-itchy periphery lesion on his trunk as given in image below, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Christmas tree appearance of Pityriasis rosea
Grattage test positive for Seborrheic Dermatitis
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows collarette of scales showing cigarette paper scaling.
• This is seen in Pityriasis rosea.
• This condition is associated with red, scaly rashes on skin.
• It may be triggered due to infections.
• The rash of pityriasis rosea spreads in a symmetrical and elongated manner along the skin's lines of cleavage (Langer's lines), resembling the shape of a Christmas tree.

Other Options:
Option B: Wickham's Striae of Lichen Planus are white lacy reticular patterns. It appears as fine, white or greyish lines or dots on the surface of the affected skin or mucous membranes.
Option C: Woronoff ring of Psoriasis is incorrect for this clinical stem. It is associated with well-defined, thick, red or silvery plaques that are often symmetrically distributed on the elbows, knees, scalp, lower back and other areas.
Option D: Grattage test positive for Seborrheic Dermatitis. This is false, as it is positive for psoriasis.

stem:
Assertion: A 40 year patient with flaccid bulla and suprabasal acantholysis.
Reason: Row of tombstone appearance seen in pemphigus vulgaris
options: Both assertion and reason are true and reason is correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are true and reason is not a correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is false and reason is true
Both assertion and reason are false
subject: Dermatology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Flaccid bullae means bulla is formed within epidermis.
• Suprabasal acantholysis is seen in pemphigus vulgaris.
• Thus, the diagnosis is pemphigus vulgaris.
• The clinical features include blistering on the skin and also on the mucosal surfaces of the oral cavity, nose, throat and even genital regions.
• The reason for pemphigus vulgaris is defect in desmoglein 3 & in later stages desmoglein 1 too.
• Row of tombstone appearance is seen histologically in pemphigus vulgaris. This is true, but not a correct explanation for assertion.

Other Options:
Option A, C and D: The other options are not correct descriptions of the given statements.

stem: Identify the lines depicted in the image given below.
subject: Dermatology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows Langer lines.
• These are skin tension lines (also called as lines of cleavage) as depicted in the image.
• These lines represent the orientation of the underlying fibres of collagen.
• These are of surgical importance as the incision is meant to be parallel to these lines. This leads to better wound healing and less scarring.

Other Options:
Option A: Blaschko lines are invisible self development lines.
Option C: Dermatomal lines are used to divide the body into dermatomes (area of skin innervated by one cutaneous nerve). They do not cross the midline.
Option D: Martorelli lines are seen in lips of mouth and the skin.

stem: During examination of an alcoholic patient who smells of alcohol, he has red eyes (conjunctival haemorrhage), he is conscious, his motor coordination is normal as he can walk in a straight line & can talk normally. Which of the following statements is true?
options: Patient has taken alcohol and is under influence of it
Patient has taken alcohol and not under its influence.
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• The patient has consumed alcohol (presence of alcoholic smell).
• The red eyes can also be attributed to alcohol intake.
• However, the patient does not exhibit any sign of derangement of mental functions or cerebellar functions.
• He is conscious and his speech is normal.
• His motor coordination and balance are also normal.
• Thus, he has consumed alcohol, but is not under the influence.

Other Options:
Option A: Patient has not taken alcohol - this is false due to the smell of alcohol and red eyes.
Option B: Patient has consumed alcohol - this is true, but it does not specify whether he is under the influence or not.
Option C: Patient has taken alcohol and is under influence of it - this is false as the patient does not exhibit any sign of derangement of mental functions or cerebellar functions.

stem: Mr. A has been in a low mood since past few months and decreased personal hygiene, on examination his thoughts were: I'm dead I don't have a head how will I eat? He is suffering from which delusion?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Delusion is a false, firm and fixed belief that persists even after evidence against it is provided.
• Delusion of nihilism is associated with denial of a patient's own existence and the world. He considers himself a dead man walking.
• Delusion of nihilism is found in severe depression with psychotic symptoms.
• Cotard delusion is the severe form of nihilistic delusion.

Other Options:
Option A: Paranoid delusion or delusion of persecution are those in which the person thinks that other people are planning to harm him in some way.
Option C: Bizarre delusions are those which are not theoretically possible. Delusion of nihilism is a bizarre delusion, but this option is not appropriate as it is not specific.
Option D: Cotard delusion is the severe form of nihilistic delusion. It could be considered as the correct option, but option B is more appropriate as it is generalised.

stem: Tactile hallucinations seen with use of cocaine are known as:
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Cocaine induced psychosis is associated with tactile hallucinations called formication.
• The person feels the sensation of 'bugs or insects crawling under the skin' (hallucination).
• Thus, option A is correct.
• Cocaine induced psychosis is also associated with paranoid delusions.

Other Options:
Option B: Dipsomania is referred to as the intermittent bouts of intense craving for alcohol consumption.
Option C: Run amok is seen with cannabis usage. The person starts behaving violently and doing rash acts.
Option D: Oneroid state is associated with dream like disturbance of one's consciousness.

stem: La belle indifference is seen in
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• La belle indifference is a mismatch between symptoms and associated distress.
• It is seen in conversion disorder or dissociative disorder.
• It is referred to as the absence of psychological disturbance despite having a serious medical condition.
• Patient talks about motor (blindness, aphonia, paralysis) and sensory (Paresthesia, Anaesthesia) symptoms but associated distress is not proportional to it.

Other Options:
Option B: Mania is the state of elevated mood for a period more than 7 days.
Option C: Schizophrenia is a psychotic disorder associated with delusion, hallucination, disorganised speech and negative symptoms.
Option D: Depression is a mood disorder in which there is depressed mood for at least 2 weeks.

stem: SCOFF criterion questionnaire is used in the evaluation for?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• The SCOFF criterion questionnaire is a screening test for assessing if a patient has an eating disorder.
• It stands for:
1. Do you make yourself Sick because you feel uncomfortably full?
2. Do you feel sad that you have lost Control on how much you eat?
3. Have you lost more than One stone (14 lb) in a three-month period?
4. Do you think yourself to be Fat when others say you are very thin?
5. Will you say that Food dominates your life?
• Two or more of these features indicates that the patient needs to be evaluated for an eating disorder.

Other Options:
Option B, C and D: Sexual disorders, behavioural disorders and mental health are not screened by the SCOFF questionnaire.

stem:
Which of the following are reversible causes of dementia?
1. Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
2. Hypothyroidism
3. Lewy Body Dementia
4. Vitamin B12 deficiency
subject: Medicine, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
The reversible causes of dementia include:
• 1. Niacin deficiency
• 2. Vitamin B12 deficiency
• 3. Infections
• 4. Toxins
• 5. Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
• 6. Hypothyroidism

• These causes can be reversed and with the reversal of aetiology, the dementia also gets resolved.

Other Options:
Option A: Vitamin B12 deficiency is also a reversible cause for dementia.
Options B and C: Lewy Body Dementia is not a reversible cause for dementia.

stem:
Which of the following is included in the criteria of substance dependence?
1: Gradually increased amount of drug taken over time
2: Consuming despite physical harm
3: Tolerance
4: Prefers substance above other activities
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
The criteria for substance dependence include:
• 1. Gradually increased amount of drug taken over time.
• 2. Consuming despite physical harm.
• 3. Tolerance.
• 4. Prefers substance above other activities.
• 5. Intense desire to take substance (craving).
• 6. Loss of control in initiation, maintenance and termination.

• All the given statements are true among the mentioned criteria.
• Thus, option D is the most appropriate answer.

Other Options:
Option A: 2,3,4 - 1 is also a criterion for substance dependence.
Option B: 1,3,4 - 2 is also a criterion for substance dependence.
Option C: 1,2,4 - 3 is also a criterion for substance dependence.

stem: A patient presents with painful bilateral hip joints and pigmented lesions on the skin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Orthopaedics, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The X-ray shows 'Shepherd Crook deformity'.
• The shepherd crook deformity is the coxa vara angulation of the femur (proximal end).
• It is seen in fibrous dysplasia.
• Pigmented skin lesions may refer to Cafe au lait spots.
• The most likely diagnosis is McCune Albright Syndrome, which is characterised by fibrous dysplasia (bone), precocious puberty and skin pigmentation (Cafe au lait spots).

Other Options:
Option A: Papillary carcinoma with bone mets shows blown out metastasis (expansile lesions).
Option C: NF1 with bony lesions shows pseudoarthrosis characterised by bowing of the bone.
Option D: Langerhans cell histiocytosis shows lytic lesions in skull with bevelled edges.

stem: Which of the following is a sign for malignant transformation of the tumour shown in the x-ray?
options: Normally it grows away from the joint, but if it grows towards the joint.
Bone marrow continuity to the tumour and parent bone
Development abnormality that causes to grow beyond skeletal maturity
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• The X Ray shows a tumour arising from the surface of the bone.
• Thus, it is a juxta-cortical bone tumour.
• These include osteochondroma and parosteal osteosarcoma.
• In osteochondroma, the cortex of the parent bone is continuous with the cortex of the tumour. It is not a sign of malignant transformation.
• Osteochondroma grows away from the joint usually, but growing towards the joint is not a sign of malignant transformation.
• Increase in cartilaginous cap thickness> 2cm is a sign of malignant transformation.
• Thus, option A is correct.
• Other features include pain, increase in size of lesion, new lucency in tumour and cortical destruction.

Other Options:
Option B: Osteochondroma grows away from the joint usually, but growing towards the joint is not a sign of malignant transformation.
Option C: In osteochondroma, the cortex of the parent bone is continuous with the cortex of the tumour. It is not a sign of malignant transformation.
Option D: Development abnormality that causes to grow beyond skeletal maturity is also not a sign of malignant transformation.

stem: Identify the MRCP finding:
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• MRCP imaging is heavily T2 weighted, so water appears white.
• Thus the white structure is bile.
• The image shows multiple filling defects (appear black) in the common bile duct.
• These are stones in the common bile duct.
• It also shows a dilatation of CBD (>6mm).
• Considering these findings, Choledocholithiasis is the most likely option.
• Thus, option B is correct.

Other Options:
Option A: Acute cholecystitis shows thickened and edematous wall of the gallbladder and pericholecystic fluid.
Option C: Pancreatic duct stones is not associated with the findings given above.
Option D: Cholangitis shows restricted diffusion of wall of CBD in diffusion weighted imaging.

stem: Which ribs are fractured?
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The counting of ribs has to be done from the first rib, near the medial end of the clavicle.
• The given X-ray shows the fracture of the 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th and 8th ribs.
• Thus, option B is correct.
• The rib fractures are caused by trauma (common), osteo arthritis, periosteal reactions and sclerotic lesions in the bones.
• The clinical features of rib fractures include pain over the chest region, bruising on the skin, swelling and tenderness around the fractured ribs.

Other Options:
Option A, C and D: The other options do not perfectly true about the fractured ribs (Ribs 4,5,6,7 and 8 are fractured).

stem: A patient presents with Dyuria, Fatigue and Backache. X-ray abdomen is shown below. What could be the likely diagnosis?
subject: Radiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The X-ray shows multiple calcifications in the renal medulla.
• It is likely to be renal medullary nephrocalcinosis.
• This leads to clinical features like dysuria, fatigue and backache.
• Renal medullary nephrocalcinosis is seen with parathyroid adenoma.
• This leads to increase in parathormone (PTH) release, which causes deranged metabolism of calcium, leading to renal stones.
• Thus, option A is correct.

Other Options:
Option B: Cystic Fibrosis is associated with thick, copious sputum due to a defect in CFTR gene.
Option C: Wilson's disease is associated with hepatolenticular degeneration and Kayser-Fleischer (KF) rings in the cornea.
Option D: Addison's disease is associated with dyselectrolytemia (hyponatremia and hyperkalemia) and brown pigmentation of the skin.

stem: Banana sign on antenatal USG is seen in:
subject: Radiology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows banana sign in antenatal ultrasonography (USG).
• This occurs because the cerebellum wraps around the brainstem.
• It is seen in Spina bifida, associated with herniation of posterior fossa structures.
• There is also a lemon shaped skull, as seen in the image.
• Thus, option A is most likely.

Other Options:
Option B: Omphalocele shows herniation of bowels through anterior abdominal wall in an antenatal USG.
Option C: Down syndrome doesn't show banana sign in antenatal USG. It shows nuchal translucency in USG.
Option D: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia doesn't show banana sign in antenatal USG. It shows cardiomediastinal shift, polyhydramnios and abnormal cardiac axis.

stem: A 50 year-old diabetic came with palpitation. On examination BP is 120/70. ECG is shown below. What is the clinical diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• A 50 year-old patient with complaints of palpitations, normotensive on examination and the given ECG shows Indiscernible P waves. P waves can also be seen inverted in the inferior limb leads (II, III, aVF) - these findings, including the ones further mentioned, are indicative of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT).
• Heart rate: Calculating the heart rate from the ECG (300÷number of large squares between consecutive R waves) gives us approx. 150 beats/minute, indicating tachycardia. PSVT is characterised by sudden onset of a rapid heart rate, typically 150 bpm or higher.
• Regular rhythm: PSVT often presents as a regular rhythm on ECG, showing uniformity in the R-R intervals, as can be seen in the ECG given.
• Narrow QRS complex: The QRS complex, representative of the electrical depolarisation of the ventricles, is narrow in PSVT.
• Absent P waves: The rapid heart rate originating at a point above the ventricles, often the AV node, result in the P waves being absent or buried within the QRS complex, making them indiscernible.
• PSVT can be caused by AV nodal reentry tachycardia (AVNRT), atrioventricular reentry or atrial tachycardia that occurs due to the presence of multiple pathways for conduction of electrical signals.

Other Options:
Option B: Ventricular tachycardia on ECG typically shows a wide QRS complex, suggesting abnormal electrical conduction through the ventricles. ... This cannot be seen in the given ECG making it unlikely that it is a case of VT.
Option C: Ventricular fibrillation ... This cannot be seen in the ECG ...
Option D: Atrial fibrillation ... The ECG given doesn't show the aforementioned signs, making it unlikely to be AF.

stem: Which of the following is not used in the treatment of hyperkalemia?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Magnesium sulphate is not typically used in the treatment of hyperkalemia.
• The treatment of hyperkalemia involves stabilising cardiac membrane potential, shifting potassium into cells and enhancing potassium excretion.
• Medical management can have a mild potassium-lowering effect by modulating the activity of the sodium-potassium pump, but is less effective and slower in action than other therapies that are more specific for the management of hyperkalemia.
• It can be administered if there is concurrent magnesium deficiency or if the patient has certain types of cardiac arrhythmias.

Other Options:
Option A: Calcium gluconate is used to stabilise cardiac cell membranes and lowers the levels of potassium, reducing the risk of fatal arrhythmias.
Option B: Insulin helps shift potassium from extracellular space into cells. Glucose is administered along with Insulin to prevent hypoglycemia that may be caused by Insulin.
Option C: Salbutamol is a beta-2 agonist which stimulates cellular potassium uptake, helping treat hyperkalemia.

stem: A 73-year-old male patient who has a history of Diabetes presents to the hospital. BP is 80/50 mm Hg. Investigations reveal serum sodium to be 145 meq/L and urine is negative for Ketones. Physical Examination shows dry tongue. Which of the fluids is to be chosen for management?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• A diabetic patient has presented with hypotension and hypovolemia (BP 80/50, dry tongue on examination) with normal sodium levels. Moreover, urine ketones are negative making it unlikely to be diabetic ketoacidosis.
• The primary goal in managing this patient is to address the volume depletion while maintaining sodium levels.
• The fluid of choice will be Isotonic saline or 0.9% sodium chloride or normal saline, to restore intravascular volume and address hypovolemia, this helps improve organ perfusion and corrects the hemodynamic instability.

Other Options:
Option A: Ringer's lactate is not the IV fluid of choice to correct hypovolemia as it is not as much of an Isotonic solution as NS ...
Option C: N/2 or 0.45% NS is not the IV fluid of choice to correct hypovolemia as it is a hypotonic solution ...
Option D: Plain water via Ryle's tube is not the fluid of choice to correct hypovolemia as it is a hypotonic solution ...

stem: A patient has vomiting and no proper dietary intake for the past 4 weeks. He was treated aggressively with IV fluids (normal saline), following which the patient developed quadriplegia and became mute. Which one of the following is a correct statement?
options: It is due to rapid correction of hyponatremia
It is due to slow correction of hyponatremia
It is due to inadequate fluid resuscitation
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has had vomiting and an improper diet for 4 weeks, which has most likely resulted in hypovolemic hyponatremia.
• When hyponatremia develops over a prolonged time, the brain adapts to the lower sodium concentration by decreasing the osmolytes within the brain cells.
• This adaptation helps maintain osmotic balance and prevents brain cells from swelling. However, if hyponatremia is corrected too rapidly, which is the case in this patient, the osmotic shift caused by the sudden increase in extracellular sodium levels can cause damage to the myelin sheaths, causing osmotic demyelination syndrome.
• Osmotic demyelination syndrome causes severe neurological deficits, as can be seen in this patient, such as dysarthria or difficulty speaking, dysphagia, weakness and in very severe cases, quadriplegia or paralysis of all four limbs.
• This can be prevented by gradual and controlled correction of hyponatremia.

Other Options:
Option B: This patient has been vomiting ... making it unlikely that he would develop hypernatremia ...
Option C: As mentioned in the case, the patient was aggressively treated with IV fluids ...
Option D: Since the patient has been vomiting ... making it unlikely that he would develop hypernatremia.

stem: Resection of all of the following structures results in macrocytosis, except?
subject: Physiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Resection of the duodenum, the proximal part of the small intestine does not result in macrocytosis.
• Macrocytosis is typically associated with specific underlying conditions such as vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, alcohol abuse, certain medications or certain types of anaemia.
• The duodenum plays a role in the absorption of several nutrients, including iron, calcium and fat-soluble vitamins like A, D, E and K. It is not, however, the primary site for the absorption of vitamin B12 or folate.

Other Options:
Option A: Resection of the ileum, the distal part of the small intestine, can lead to macrocytosis as it would impair the absorption of vitamin B12, which occurs predominantly in the distal part of the ileum.
Option B: Resection of the stomach can lead to macrocytosis as it plays a role in the initial stages of digestion and release of intrinsic factor, necessary for vitamin B12 absorption.
Option C: Resection of the jejunum, the middle portion of the small intestine, can lead to macrocytosis (as it plays a crucial role in nutrient absorption) due to deficiencies of vitamin B12 and folate.

stem: Lesion in which of the following structures give rise to hemiballismus?
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The subthalamic nucleus (STN), part of the basal ganglia, is involved in motor control and movement regulation.
• Hemiballismus is a rare movement disorder characterised by involuntary and aggressive flinging or jerking or flailing movements of one side of the body. These movements are typically large and can be forceful, leading to difficulty with normal motor control and coordination.
• The STN is implicated in the development of hemiballismus due to its role in motor circuitry and its connections with other structures in the basal ganglia.
• Lesions or disturbances in the STN can lead to an imbalance in the basal ganglia-thalamocortical circuitry, resulting in the abnormal movements seen in hemiballismus.

Other Options:
Option A: The caudate nucleus, a component of the basal ganglia, plays a role in motor control and coordination. However, damage to it is more commonly associated with other movement disorders such as Huntington's disease or chorea, rather than hemiballismus.
Option B: The putamen, a component of the basal ganglia, plays a role in motor control but is not associated with the development of hemiballismus. Dysfunction of the putamen can contribute to other movement disorders such as Parkinson's disease or Huntington's disease.
Option D: The midbrain, located between the forebrain and hindbrain, consists of substantia nigra, red nucleus and superior colliculus. Disturbances in the substantia nigra can lead to abnormal movements, but it is not the whole of the midbrain that is involved and it is less likely to cause hemiballismus than the STN.

stem: A patient complains of difficulty in breathing and the treating physician makes a diagnosis of an inherited condition in which thick mucus is built up in lungs and pancreas. Which of the following electrolyte ions and gene mutation is involved in this inherited condition?
options: Chloride ion and deletion of phenylalanine at 508 position
Chloride ion and deletion of tryptophan at 508 position
Chloride ion and deletion of phenylalanine at 708 position
Chloride ion and deletion of leucine at 508 position
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• An inherited condition in which there is build up of thick mucus in the lungs and pancreas is cystic fibrosis (CF).
• The gene mutation affects the CFTR gene which plays an important role in ion transport across cell membranes.
• The mutation leads to a dysfunctional or absent CFTR protein, resulting in impaired ion regulation and fluid balance in various organs.
• The primary electrolyte ions affected in CF are chloride and sodium. The CFTR protein normally acts as a chloride channel, facilitating the movement of chloride ions out of cells.
• However, in CF, the defective CFTR protein disrupts chloride transport, leading to reduced chloride secretion and increased absorption of sodium across cell membranes.
• The deletion of phenylalanine at position 508 of the CFTR protein results in a misfolded and dysfunctional CFTR protein, affecting the regulation of chloride and other ions across cell membranes. It is associated with a severe form of CF and is the most common CFTR mutation.

Other Options:
Option B: Chloride ion transport is affected, however, there is no known deletion of tryptophan at the 508 position in any specific gene or protein that leads to CF.
Option C: Chloride ion transport is affected, however, there is no known deletion of phenylalanine at the 708 position, it is the 508 position that is affected in CF.
Option D: Chloride ion transport is affected, however, there is no known deletion of leucine at the 508 position in any gene or protein that leads to CF.

stem: Which of the following is seen in primary hypothyroidism?
options: FT4: low, FT3: low, RT3: normal, TOTAL T4: normal, TOTAL T3: normal, TSH: elevated
FT4: normal, FT3: low, RT3: elevated, TOTAL T4: normal, TOTAL T3: normal, TSH: normal
FT4: high, FT3: high, RT3: normal, TOTAL T4: high, TOTAL T3: high, TSH: low
FT4: high, FT3: high, RT3: normal, TOTAL T4: normal, TOTAL T3: normal, TSH: elevated
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Primary hypothyroidism is a condition where the thyroid gland is unable to produce sufficient thyroid hormones, affecting the levels of various aspects of the thyroid function tests.
• FT4 or free T4 is decreased. It represents the levels of thyroid hormone circulating in the bloodstream.
• FT3 or free T3 is decreased. It represents the active form of the thyroid hormone.
• RT3 or reverse T3 can be normal or elevated. It is an inactive form of the thyroid hormone that is produced in certain conditions, during stress or an illness.
• Total T4 levels may be normal or decreased. It includes both active and inactive forms of the hormone bound to proteins.
• Total T3 levels may be normal or decreased. It includes both active and inactive forms bound to proteins.
• TSH or thyroid stimulating hormone levels are typically elevated. TSH is produced by the anterior pituitary in response to low levels of thyroid hormones. Increased TSH levels indicate that the pituitary is trying to stimulate the thyroid gland to produce more thyroid hormones.

Other Options:
Option B: FT4 would not be normal and RT3 would not be elevated, as there is insufficient production of thyroid hormones. Additionally, TSH would be elevated as the pituitary would respond to the low levels of thyroid hormones by producing TSH.
Option C: FT4, FT3, total T4 and total T3 would be decreased and not elevated, as there is insufficient production of thyroid hormones. Additionally, TSH would be elevated as the pituitary would respond to low levels of thyroid hormones by producing TSH.
Option D: FT4 and FT3 levels would be decreased and not elevated, as there is insufficient production of thyroid hormones.

stem: A 40 year old patient presents to the clinic with hoarseness of voice, protrusion of chin, excessive sweating, enlargement of hands and lower limbs and was recently diagnosed with diabetes. Which of the following drugs (growth hormone antagonist) is used to treat this condition?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The symptoms described in the case scenario are suggestive of acromegaly.
• Acromegaly is a rare hormonal disorder caused by excessive production of growth hormone (GH) by the pituitary gland, commonly due to a benign tumour called a pituitary adenoma.
• The excessive GH secretion leads to the enlargement of body tissues, including the hands, feet, face and internal organs.
• Acromegaly can also lead to impaired glucose tolerance and metabolism, leading to the development of diabetes in this patient.
• The treatment of choice is pegvisomant, a medication used to manage acromegaly. It acts by blocking the effects of GH (receptor antagonism) on body tissues, helping control the symptoms and reduce the size of the tumour.

Other Options:
Option B: Octreotide, a synthetic analogue of somatostatin, a hormone that inhibits the release of GH is not typically used as a primary therapy to treat acromegaly. It may be used as adjunctive treatment instead.
Option C: Cabergoline, a dopamine receptor agonist is primarily used to treat hyperprolactinemia and Parkinson's disease. It can suppress GH secretion to a certain extreme, but it is not as effective as other medications.
Option D: Olcegepant, a calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist, is used for the treatment of acute migraine attacks. It doesn't have a role in regulating GH secretion.

stem: Deep Y descent in JVP is seen all of the following conditions, except
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Cardiac tamponade results due to excessive fluid or blood build-up in the pericardial sac, putting pressure on the heart, limiting its ability to fill and effectively pump blood. The restricted filling of the heart affects the dynamics of the jugular venous pressure (JVP) waveform.
• The y descent represents rapid emptying of blood from the RA into the RV during diastole.
• In cardiac tamponade, the elevated pressure in the pericardial sac acts as counter-pressure against the RA, impairing its ability to effectively empty blood into the RV during diastole.
• As a result, the y descent in the JVP waveform is diminished or absent in cardiac tamponade. The JVP waveform, instead, exhibits a "square root sign" appearance.

Other Options:
Option B: Constrictive pericarditis, involving inflammation and thickening of the pericardium and restricted normal expansion of the heart during diastole, leads to a prominent or deep y descent as blood rapidly empties from the RA into the RC when the tricuspid valve opens.
Option C: Restrictive cardiomyopathy, characterised by impaired ventricular filling due to the stiffening of the myocardium, involves impaired filling of the ventricles during diastole causing a deep y descent in the JVP waveform.
Option D: Tricuspid regurgitation, referring to backflow of blood from the RV into the RA during systole, causes an exaggerated y descent due to regurgitant blood rapidly filling the RA during diastole.

stem: In a patient, the plasma sodium concentration was found to be 145 mmol/L, chloride concentration was 80 mmol/L, bicarbonate concentration was 30 mmol/L and intracellular potassium concentration was 4 mmol/L. Calculate the anion gap in this patient
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The anion gap represents the difference between the measured cations (Na+) and measured anions (Cl- + HCO3-) in the plasma.
• The anion gap can be calculated using the formula:
• Anion Gap = [Na+] - ([Cl-] + [HCO3-])
• Given the following values:
• [Na+] = 145 mmol/L
• [Cl-] = 80 mmol/L
• [HCO3-] = 30 mmol/L
• Anion Gap = 145 - (80 + 30)
• = 145 - 110 = 35 mmol/L
• Therefore, the anion gap in this case is 35 mmol/L

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: As per the formulae and calculations thus employed, these options are not correct.

stem:
A patient has been having severe vomiting since the past 2 days. He also develops orthostatic hypotension. Which of the following will be seen?
1. Hypokalemia
2. Hypochloremia
3. Metabolic Alkalosis
4. Respiratory Alkalosis
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• This patient is experiencing a fluid and electrolyte imbalance, due to severe vomiting for two days and consequential orthostatic hypotension.
• Severe vomiting can lead to loss of HCl from the stomach, causing a decrease in the body's overall chloride levels. This leads to hypochloremia.
• The loss of chloride can lead to volume depletion, as chloride is an essential component of the extracellular fluid. This volume depletion and hypochloremia, makes the kidneys conserve sodium (Na+) and excrete hydrogen ions (H+). This involves the reabsorption of bicarbonate (HCO3-) by the kidneys, causing an increased concentration of bicarbonate in the blood. This contributes to metabolic alkalosis.
• As hydrogen ions (H+) are excreted in exchange for sodium (Na+), there is an increased excretion of potassium (K+) in the urine, leading to hypokalemia. Vomiting itself contributes to potassium loss due to the loss of gastric secretions rich in potassium.

Other Options:
Options B and C: Respiratory acidosis and not alkalosis would develop due to reduced tissue perfusion, increased anaerobic metabolism and impaired renal compensatory mechanisms.
Option D: In addition to hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis, there will also be hypochloremia due to loss of chloride via severe vomiting.

stem: Which of the following would be the specific antibody found in dermatomyositis?
subject: Dermatology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune inflammatory myopathy, characterised by muscle weakness, skin rash and involvement of other organs.
• The presence of anti Mi-2 antibodies in the blood is considered a hallmark feature of dermatomyositis. It is highly specific and the most common antibody associated with this condition.
• The presence of anti Mi-2 antibodies is often associated with certain clinical features of dermatomyositis, such as Gottron's papules and heliotrope rash. It is also associated with muscle involvement.
• These antibodies can be detected using blood tests, such as ELISA or immunoblotting techniques.

Other Options:
Option A: Antinuclear antibodies (ANA) are associated with several autoimmune conditions, including SLE, rheumatoid arthritis and Sjogren's syndrome. They are not specific or sensitive for dermatomyositis.
Option B: Antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are associated with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC). They are not specific or sensitive for dermatomyositis.
Option C: Anticentromere antibodies are associated with systemic sclerosis or scleroderma. They are not specific or sensitive for dermatomyositis.

stem: Which of the following is true about Caplan syndrome?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Caplan syndrome, also known as rheumatoid pneumoconiosis, is a rare condition characterised by a combination of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and pneumoconiosis.
• Pneumoconiosis is a lung disease caused by inhalation of dust particles, seen in coal miners.
• In Caplan syndrome, individuals with a history of exposure to coal dust or other mineral dusts develop characteristic lung nodules, called rheumatoid nodules, in addition to the joint inflammation seen in RA.
• Diagnosis involves clinical evaluation, imaging studies such as chest X-ray and CT scan and assessment of the characteristic nodules.
• Treatment aims to manage the symptoms of RA and to provide supportive care for lung function and respiratory symptoms.

Other Options:
Option B: Pleural effusion is not a typical feature of Caplan syndrome, rather, there is development of rheumatoid nodules in the lungs.
Option C: Caplan syndrome involves the development of lung nodules that typically affect the upper lobes of the lungs which distinguishes it from other forms of pneumoconiosis, although they can occur in other lung regions as well.
Option D: HRCT in Caplan syndrome reveals multiple, well-defined nodules in the lung parenchyma. The nodules are often round or oval-shaped and may vary in size. Nodules may be scattered throughout the lung parenchyma, typically in the upper lobes. They are dense and may have a ground-glass or halo-like appearance surrounding them. It doesn't show a crazy-paving pattern as can be seen in ARDS, diffuse alveolar damage, pulmonary edema and other interstitial lung diseases.

stem: A 53 year old woman is being evaluated for daytime sleepiness and memory loss which have been present for the past 1 year. Her BMI is 43, her BP is 170/98 mmHg. In an awake state, ABG shows pO2 70 mmHg, pCO2 53 mmHg, HCO3 33 mmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has a BMI of >30, categorised as obesity. Her blood pressure is elevated and her ABG is indicative of respiratory acidosis and metabolic alkalosis.
• The most likely diagnosis, therefore, is that of obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS), a respiratory disorder that occurs in individuals with obesity. It is characterised by obesity, hypoventilation (pO2 70 mmHg) and chronic daytime hypercapnia (pCO2 53 mmHg).
• It is caused due to the mechanical factors of weight and fat that restrict the movement of the chest and diaphragm. Additionally, obesity can disrupt the response to increased carbon dioxide levels which further hampers the ventilation.
• The patient may also experience obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), a condition characterised by repeated episodes of partial or complete upper airway collapse during sleep.
• Treatment involves lifestyle modifications like weight loss and increased physical activity, continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy to treat apnea and improve ventilation during sleep.

Other Options:
Option A: Obstructive sleep apnea, a sleep-related breathing disorder, presents in patients with obesity as poor sleep, snoring or apneic episodes while sleeping and daytime sleepiness. This patient has not complained of these symptoms hence it is unlikely to be OSA.
Option C: Central sleep apnea is a sleep-related breathing disorder characterised by repeated interruptions in breathing while sleeping due to a disruption in the brain's respiratory control centre. It can be caused due to brainstem abnormalities, opioids and sedatives among other causes. This patient doesn't have signs or symptoms of CSA.
Option D: Alzheimer's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that primarily affects the brain, leading to cognitive decline, memory loss and changes in thinking abilities and behaviour. This patient doesn't have the typical signs or symptoms of Alzheimer's disease.

stem:
A 35 year old man, with no significant past medical history, is being treated for severe breathlessness. He is febrile. His SpO2 is 87% on 6L oxygen, with nasal cannula. He is oriented only to person. TLC is 19k. Serum Creatinine is 1.45 mg/dL. Sputum shows 2+ pus cells and 1+ epithelial cells. Rhinovirus is demonstrated in the anterior nares. Which of the following should be administered?
1. Ceftazidime + Azithromycin
2. Ceftriaxone + Levofloxacin
3. Piperacillin/Tazobactam + Ciprofloxacin
4. Azithromycin alone
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has developed a moderately severe case of community acquired pneumonia as there is fever, a significantly raised leucocyte count (19,000) and 2+ pus cells in sputum. In addition to this, there is a concurrent rhinovirus infection as well.
• As per the guidelines, empirical treatment must be started for this patient and he must be admitted.
• Outpatients are usually administered a macrolide (clarithromycin or azithromycin), if allergic, then doxycycline.
• Inpatients not requiring ICU are administered a respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin or moxifloxacin) and a beta-lactam (ceftriaxone, ampicillin, cefotaxime, ertapenem) plus a macrolide (clarithromycin or azithromycin).
• Inpatients requiring ICU are administered a beta lactam (ceftriaxone, ampicillin-sulbactam, cefotaxime) in addition to either azithromycin or a fluoroquinolone.
• As the patient's condition is not very stable, oxygen levels not well-maintained on 6L oxygen, he may require an admission into the ICU.
• Hence, out of the given options, the best options for broad-based empirical treatment are ceftazidime + azithromycin and ceftriaxone + levofloxacin. This may need to be changed once culture reports become available.

Other Options:
Options B and C: Piperacillin/Tazobactam + Ciprofloxacin are reserved for treatment of pseudomonas infections. Since the culture reports and infectious agent causing the pneumonia are not mentioned, this is not the treatment of choice in this patient.
Option D: Piperacillin/Tazobactam + Ciprofloxacin are reserved for treatment of pseudomonas infections. Additionally, azithromycin would not suffice as this patient will be admitted and treated, hence, as per guidelines, other empirical treatment such as a beta-lactam, a fluoroquinolone will be required as well.

stem: Which of the following does not qualify as severe COVID?
subject: Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• HRCT is a highly sensitive modality in patients affected by SARS-CoV-2. It has been a widely used tool to aid diagnosis and planning treatment plans.
• Studies have shown that the risk of death due to severe COVID is significantly increased in patients with a higher CT score on a 24-day follow-up period. It also confirmed that CT assessment accurately reflects short-term outcomes.
• However, an involvement of 42% would not be categorised as severe COVID.
• In patients with severe COVID, the lung involvement will be more than 50%.

Other Options:
Option A: The normal respiratory rate is 12-20 breaths per minute. Hence, a RR of 32/min would be classified as severe COVID-related tachypnoea.
Option B: The normal SpO2 of a patient with no other lung conditions is >95%. Hence, a SpO2 of 87% on room air would classify as significant hypoxia and hence, severe COVID.
Option D: In severe COVID, there can be a fall in blood pressure and the mean arterial blood pressure (MAP) can fall below 60-70 mmHg.

stem: In a patient with aortic regurgitation, the murmur is likely to be heard is:
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Normally, the aortic valve opens at the end of the isovolumetric contraction phase of the systole, allowing the ejection of blood from the left ventricle (LV) to the aorta, closing at the end of ejection.
• If it fails to close properly, it leads to regurgitation of blood from the aorta to the LV, producing an early diastolic murmur. This murmur is best heard in the isovolumetric relaxation phase.
• After the isovolumetric relaxation, the mitral valve opens and rapid filling begins, during which the LV also gets filled from the left atrium, leading to a quick rise in LV pressure and this leads to LV pressure exceeding aortic pressure, stopping the regurgitation.
• Due to the aforementioned, the murmur of aortic regurgitation (AR) is restricted to the early phase of diastole, heard as a blowing, decrescendo murmur.

Other Options:
Option A: AR murmur is not typically heard throughout the entire cardiac cycle and this is because it is primarily heard during early diastole and diminishes during systole as the flow also diminishes, reducing the murmur intensity.
Option C: During isovolumetric contraction, both the aortic and mitral valves are closed, there is no significant flow of blood across the aortic valve, therefore, the murmur of AR is not heard during this phase of the cardiac cycle.
Option D: During the rapid filling phase of the cardiac cycle, the mitral valve opens, left ventricular filling leads to a rise in LV pressure, stopping the regurgitation from the aortic valve into the LV, which is why the murmur is not best heard during this phase.

stem:
Consider the following areas for CVS Examination
1. Pulmonary
2. Aortic
3. Mitral
During the physical examination, what is the sequence (superior to inferior) of these areas, on the left chest wall?
subject: Medicine, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• During physical examination of the cardiovascular system, the sequence of the areas on the left chest wall, from superior to inferior would be as follows:
• Pulmonary -> Aortic -> Mitral
• The pulmonary area is located in the 2nd intercostal space along the left sternal border and corresponds to the position of the pulmonary valve. It is the highest point of examination on the left side.
• The aortic area is located in the 2nd intercostal space along the right sternal border and corresponds to the position of the aortic valve. Although it is on the right side, it is traditionally examined after the pulmonary area.
• The mitral area is located at the apex of the heart, the point of maximum impulse, on the chest wall. It is typically felt in the 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line. This area corresponds to the position of the mitral valve and is the lowest point of examination on the left side.
• This can be visualised better in the image given below.


Other Options:
Option A: The pulmonary area lies more superior and is the highest point of examination on the left side, therefore, it would come prior to the aortic area in sequence.
