Option C: The mitral area is the more inferior point of examination on the left side, hence it would come last in the given sequence and not before the pulmonary and aortic areas, respectively.
Option D: The mitral area lies more inferior than the aortic area, hence it would come after and not before the aortic area in the given sequence.

stem: A 30 year old female came to the outpatient department with the features of skin thickening and cold clammy fingers. She had complaints of pain in the fingers, when exposed to cold. On evaluation, SCL-70 was found to be positive. What would be the specific antibody associated with this disorder?
subject: Medicine, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has presented with skin thickening, cold clammy and painful fingers when exposed to cold (Raynaud's phenomenon) with SCL-70 positive. The signs, symptoms and SCL-70 positive test correspond to an autoimmune disease called scleroderma.
• Scleroderma or systemic sclerosis is a chronic autoimmune disorder characterised by fibrosis of the skin and involvement of internal organs.
• Anti-Scl antibodies are found in these patients with systemic sclerosis. The presence of these antibodies is associated with severe skin involvement and interstitial lung disease.
• Detection can be done by ELISA or immunodiffusion.

Other Options:
Option A: Anti-Jo antibodies are a type of anti-synthetase antibody associated with idiopathic inflammatory myopathies, the most common one being polymyositis.
Option C: Antinuclear antibodies (ANA) is sensitive for autoimmune diseases, including SLE, but is not specific for any particular disease.
Option D: Anti-Smith antibodies are specific to systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), a chronic autoimmune condition.

stem:
A patient was diagnosed to have iron deficiency anaemia. Which of the following could be expected in this patient?
1. Decreased serum iron
2. Decreased transferrin saturation
3. Decreased serum ferritin
4. Increased TIBC
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
A patient diagnosed with iron deficiency anaemia will have the following:
• Decreased serum iron levels: Serum iron levels correspond to the amount of iron in blood, in case of iron deficiency anaemia, these levels are reduced.
• Decreased transferrin saturation: Transferrin is a protein that binds to iron and transports it in the blood, the saturation of which represents the percentage of transferrin that is bound to iron. In iron deficiency anaemia, the transferrin saturation is reduced, because there isn't enough iron available to saturate the transferrin molecules.
• Decreased serum ferritin levels: Ferritin is an intracellular protein that stores iron and serves as an indicator of iron stores in the body. These levels are reduced, because the iron stores are depleted.
• Increased total iron-binding capacity (TIBC): TIBC is a measure of the capacity of transferrin to bind with iron. In iron deficiency anaemia, TIBC levels are increased as a response to the reduced iron levels.

Other Options:
Option A: In addition to decreased serum iron levels and decreased transferrin saturation, there will also be a decrease in the serum ferritin levels and an increase in TIBC, as mentioned above.
Option B: In addition to decreased serum iron levels and decreased ferritin levels, there will also be a decrease in the transferrin saturation and an increase in TIBC, as mentioned above.
Option C: In addition to decreased serum iron levels, decreased ferritin levels and decreased transferrin saturation, there will also be an increase in TIBC, as mentioned above.

stem:
A patient presented with dull aching pain in the tortuous veins of both limbs. Which of the following tests can be used to check the competency of SF junction?
1. Fegan
2. Morrissey
3. Modified Perthes
4. Brodie Trendelenberg
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
The Morrissey test and Brodie Trendelenberg test are specific manoeuvres used to assess the competency of the saphenofemoral junction in patients with varicose veins.
• These tests help evaluate the presence of venous reflux.
• The Morrissey test involves manual compression of the greater saphenous vein in the thigh while the patient stands. The examiner applies pressure to occlude the vein and then asks the patient to walk or perform calf muscle exercises. If the varicose veins fill from below, it suggests the presence of venous reflux.
• The Brodie Trendeleberg test is used to assess the competency of the superficial venous system, particularly the great saphenous vein. The patient is placed in a supine position, and the leg is elevated to about 60 degrees. The examiner occludes the great saphenous vein at the upper thigh, and the patient is then asked to stand up. If there is rapid filling of the varicose veins or reflux observed during this manoeuvre, it suggests incompetence of the great saphenous vein.

Other Options:
Option B: The Fegan test is used to assess the competency of the perforating veins in patients with venous insufficiency, and not to assess the competency of the SF junction.
Option C: The modified Perthes test is used to rule out deep vein thrombosis (DVT), and not to assess the competency of the SF junction.
Option D: The Fegan test and the modified Perthes test are used to assess the competency of perforators and rule out DVT, respectively, and not to assess the competency of the SF junction.

stem: Which of the following is not a perineal approach for rectal prolapse?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The Ripstein procedure, also called the Ripstein rectopexy, is a surgical technique used to treat rectal prolapse.
• It involves the placement of a synthetic mesh or a strip of polypropylene mesh around the rectum and attaching it to the sacrum to provide support and prevent further prolapse.
• It is not a perineal approach. The incision is made in the abdominal wall and the rectum is mobilised and brought into its anatomical position.

Other Options:
Option B: The Delorme procedure, known as a perineal rectosigmoidectomy, is performed through the perineum to remove excess mucosa and submucosa of the prolapsed rectum, followed by plication of the remaining rectal tissue. The edges are then sutured together to secure the rectum in its proper position.
Option C: The Thiersch procedure, also called the anal encirclement procedure, involves encircling the anus with a non-absorbable synthetic material, such as a mesh or tape. It is a perineal approach.
Option D: In Altemeier procedure, a perineal rectosigmoidectomy with rectal mucosal resection, is performed through the perineum. It involves removing the prolapsed segment and suturing the remaining healthy rectal tissue to the anal opening.

stem: A patient presents with h/o dysentery and fever for the past 6 weeks. Along with that there is associated upper abdominal pain. USG shows abscess with irregular margins in the liver. What is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Microbiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The presence of dysentery, fever, upper abdominal pain and an abscess with irregular margins on USG is suggestive of a liver abscess.
• In this case, the most common cause of liver abscess is amoebic liver abscess, caused by the protozoan parasite Entamoeba histolytica. It is typically associated with dysentery and is more prevalent in regions with poor sanitation.
• Treatment involves oral metronidazole, luminal agents like paromomycin and if it is a large abscess, surgical drainage may be advised.

Other Options:
Options B and D: Echinococcus granulosus abscess or hydatid cyst is caused by a larval tapeworm, Echinococcus granulosus. Patients experience abdominal pain, hepatomegaly and USG shows a unilocular cyst lesion with a "water lily sign". These findings are not consistent with the case mentioned here.
Option C: Pyogenic liver abscess is caused by a bacterial infection from the biliary tract or GIT. Patients experience fever, RUQ pain and lab tests show elevated inflammatory markers while USG shows a fluid-filled cavity with thick walls and possible gas formation. These findings are not consistent with the case mentioned here.

stem: Regarding breast conservation surgery, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: Post Operative radiation is not compulsory
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Postoperative radiation therapy is often necessary after breast conservation surgery (lumpectomy or partial mastectomy) for the following reasons:
• Reduces local recurrence by targeting any remaining cancer cells in the breast tissue after surgery.
• Adjuvant radiation therapy after surgery has shown to improve overall survival rates.
• Radiation therapy improves local control of the disease by minimising spread to nearby lymph nodes or surrounding tissues.

Other Options:
Option A: A wide local excision, known as a lumpectomy or partial mastectomy is performed to remove the tumour along with a surrounding margin of healthy tissue.
Option C: Intraoperative frozen section analysis during breast conservation surgery is performed to assess the margins of the excised tumour and ensure complete removal.
Option D: Axillary lymph node dissection is a surgical procedure performed during breast conservation surgery, if spread is suspected or to determine the extent of spread and also to reduce the risk of recurrence.

stem: What variant of gallbladder is shown in the USG image below?
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Phrygian cap gallbladder is a variant of gallbladder anatomy characterised by folding or kinking of the gallbladder fundus, resulting in a distinctive appearance resembling a Phrygian cap, which is a soft conical hat.
• On USG, the Phrygian cap gallbladder may appear as a prominent fold or bend in the fundus, as can be seen in the USG image given, giving it a partially or completely divided appearance. The remaining portions of the gallbladder, including the neck and cystic duct, appear normal.
• It is a benign anatomical variant, asymptomatic and doesn't require any specific treatment or intervention.

Other Options:
Option A: Duplicated gallbladder is a rare congenital anomaly where there are two separate gallbladders instead of one. On USG, it appears as two distinct gallbladder structures with separate necks and cystic ducts.
Option B: GB polyp refers to the presence of a polypoid growth or protrusion from the gallbladder wall. On USG, it appears as a localised, rounded or pedunculated structure with the gallbladder lumen.
Option D: The "Mercedes Benz sign" is seen on USG, it refers to the shape of the gallbladder, resembling the logo of the car. It has a characteristic three-lobed appearance with the main body representing the central portion and the two smaller lobes resembling the wings.

stem: A 73 year old man presents to the OPD with chronic bleeding per rectum. Which of the following tests is used for confirmation?
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This patient's age and symptoms raise a high suspicion for colorectal cancer.
• Hence, colonoscopy and contrast enhanced CT (CECT) are used to evaluate and confirm the diagnosis.
• Colonoscopy involves the insertion of a colonoscope into the rectum and colon to visualise the lining of the large intestine. It allows direct visualisation of the rectum, colon and terminal ileum. A biopsy can also be taken, which would help confirm the diagnosis.
• CECT uses contrast material to enhance the visualisation of structures and blood vessels during a CT scan. It helps identify structural abnormalities, such as tumours, diverticula or vascular abnormalities, that could be causing rectal bleeding. It also helps identify the extent of the disease and potential involvement of adjacent organs.

Other Options:
Option A: Ca 19-9 is a tumour marker that could be elevated in pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer and gastric cancer. It is not a specific test and would not help confirm the diagnosis in this patient.
Option B: CECT would help diagnose, but a colonoscopy is gold standard as it allows direct visualisation.
Option C: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a tumour marker that could be elevated in colorectal cancer, gastric cancer, lung cancer and ovarian cancer. It is not a specific test and would not help confirm the diagnosis in this patient.

stem: There was a fracture of the shaft of the femur. The patient was initially stable but gradually developed a rash over the axilla and chest in the next 12 hours, his vitals are now unstable. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Fat embolism syndrome (FES) is a rare but serious condition that may occur after long bone fractures, such as a fracture of the shaft of the femur.
• This occurs due to fat globules originating from the bone marrow or adipose tissue that induce a toxic vasculitis followed by platelet-fibrin thrombosis and actual obstruction of small pulmonary vessels by fat molecules.
• It typically presents within the first 24 to 72 hours after the initial injury. The patient may become drowsy, restless, disoriented or unconscious and have tachypnoea, tachycardia and dyspnoea. These features are seen in this patient as well.
• Additionally, the axillary petechial rash is a classic finding in FES due to microhemorrhages caused by the rupture of blood vessels due to fat emboli.
• FES is a medical emergency and can be diagnosed clinically.
• Treatment involves respiratory support, heparinisation, IV LMW dextran and corticosteroids.

Other Options:
Option B: The patient has not sustained injuries to the chest, as per information given. Since he was initially stable and has now become unstable, it is unlikely to be a blunt injury to the chest.
Option C: The patient has not sustained injuries to the chest, as per the information given. Flail chest refers to fractures of the ribs and would present with severe chest pain, making it unlikely in this patient.
Option D: Myocardial infarction presents with severe chest pain, elevated cardiac markers and is unrelated to the fracture. The patient has no symptoms of an MI.

stem: A surgeon tried to reduce the hernia without removing its content. What is it called as?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Reduction en masse occurs when a hernia sac is pushed back into the abdominal cavity without relieving the obstruction/strangulation.
• The hernial contents remain trapped within the sac, which is now inside the abdomen.
• Clinically dangerous because:
• External swelling disappears
• But intestinal obstruction/strangulation persists internally
• This situation often happens after forceful or improper reduction.

Other Options:
Option B - Maydl hernia:
• Also called "hernia-in-W"
• Involves two loops of bowel in sac with strangulation of intra-abdominal loop
• Not related to reduction
Option C - Incarceration:
• Hernia that is irreducible
• Contents are trapped but not necessarily strangulated
• No reduction attempted
Option D - Sliding hernia:
• One wall of the sac is formed by a viscus (e.g., colon, bladder)
• Anatomical type, not related to reduction

stem:
Which of the following is not seen in Intermittent Claudication?
1. Rest Pain at night
2. Pain starts on taking the first step
3. Most common cause of atheroma
4. Clinical Presentation is not related to the affected side
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Intermittent claudication is a symptom characterised by pain or discomfort in the muscles of the legs, usually the calves, that occurs during physical activity and is relieved with rest.
• It occurs due to narrowing or blockage of the arteries such as peripheral artery disease.
• Rest pain is not present in intermittent claudication, instead, it is actually relieved on rest. Rest pain is indicative of significant arterial insufficiency.
• Intermittent claudication pain is a cramping or aching pain that occurs after a certain distance or time of activity.
• While atheroma (atherosclerosis) is a common cause of intermittent claudication, it is not the most common cause. The most common cause is peripheral arterial disease.
• If the blockage is present in the arteries of the right leg, the symptoms will predominantly affect the right leg and vice versa.

Other Options:
Option A: Rest pain is not present in intermittent claudication and it occurs after a certain distance or time and activity. However, the most common cause is not atheroma and the symptoms are present on the affected side as well.
Option B: Intermittent claudication pain occurs after a certain distance or time of activity, the most common cause is not atheroma and the symptoms are present on the affected side. However, rest pain is also not present.
Option C: Intermittent claudication pain occurs after a certain distance or time of activity and the most common cause is not atheroma. However, rest pain is not present and the symptoms are related to the affected side.

stem:
Which of the following conditions has smoking as a risk factor?
1. Fibroadenoma
2. Mondor
3. Carcinoma
4. Duct Ectasia
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Smoking is a risk factor for Mondor's disease, a condition characterised by superficial thrombophlebitis of the veins beneath the skin, typically in the breast or chest wall.
• Carcinoma, of the lung, breast or abdominal organs, can be caused due to smoking, as has been shown in many studies.
• Duct ectasia, a benign breast condition characterised by dilation of the milk ducts beneath the nipple, could be associated with smoking as a risk factor as well.

Other Options:
Options A and C: Smoking is not a known risk factor for fibroadenoma, a benign tumour of the breast.
Option D: In addition to carcinoma and duct ectasia, smoking is also a risk factor for Mondor's disease.

stem: Which of the following structures is not associated with the third part of duodenum?
options: Body and trunk of the gallbladder
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The body and fundus of the trunk of the gallbladder is not in close proximity to the third part of the duodenum.
• The third part of the duodenum is the horizontal portion that follows the descending part of the duodenum and precedes the ascending part. It lies in the retroperitoneal space behind the head of the pancreas.
• The gallbladder is located on the undersurface of the liver, specifically the gallbladder fossa, which is on the visceral surface of the liver's right lobe.

Other Options:
Option A: The third part of the duodenum passes in close proximity to the left suprarenal gland, located above the upper pole of the left kidney.
Option C: The third part of the duodenum crosses anterior to the abdominal aorta.
Option D: The third part of the duodenum crosses anterior to the inferior vena cava (IVC).

stem: Which of the following is not a complication of Parenteral Nutrition?
subject: Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Aspiration is not a complication of parenteral nutrition.
• Parenteral nutrition refers to the administration of nutrients intravenously, bypassing the gastrointestinal tract.
• As such, it does not involve swallowing or the passage of food or fluids through the oesophagus, which is where the complication of aspiration would commonly occur.

Other Options:
Option A: Pneumothorax, the presence of air or gas in the pleural space that may lead to lung collapse, can occur as a complication of central venous catheter placement during administration of parenteral nutrition.
Option B: Refeeding syndrome is a metabolic complication that can occur when nutrition is reintroduced to severely malnourished individuals, including those receiving parenteral nutrition.
Option C: Osteoporosis can develop in patients with prolonged malnutrition receiving long-term parenteral nutrition. The lack of oral intake and inadequate nutrient delivery can decrease bone mineral density and increased risk of fractures.

stem: Which of the following is the most common type of Adrenal Incidentaloma?
subject: Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Non-functioning adrenal adenomas are the most common type of adrenal incidentalomas.
• These adenomas do not produce excessive hormones and are often discovered incidentally during imaging studies performed for unrelated reasons.
• Non-functioning adrenal adenomas are usually benign and do not cause symptoms or require immediate intervention.

Other Options:
Option A: A functioning adrenal adenoma is an adrenal tumour that produces excessive hormones, leading to hormonal imbalances and related symptoms. These tumours may be discovered incidentally, but are more likely to present with symptoms.
Option C: Adrenocortical carcinoma is a rare but aggressive type of cancer that is capable of producing excess hormones, or may be non-functional, but the former is more likely, making it less likely to be an incidental finding.
Option D: Pheochromocytoma is a tumour that arises from the adrenal medulla, it produces excessive catecholamines, leading to episodic hypertension, palpitations, headaches, sweating and anxiety. It is very unlikely to be an incidental finding due to these reasons.

stem: A patient presents with dysuria and flank pain which are diagnosed as renal calculi. This has been a recurrent problem with the patient. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with it?
subject: Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• In a patient with recurrent renal calculi, the most likely associated condition could be hyperparathyroidism.
• Hyperparathyroidism is a condition characterised by excessive secretion of parathyroid hormone (PTH) by the parathyroid glands, located in the neck.
• Elevated levels of PTH can lead to increased calcium levels, which can contribute to the formation of calcium-based kidney stones. These stones can obstruct the urinary tract, leading to symptoms such as dysuria and flank pain.
• Diagnosis can be confirmed by testing the serum calcium and PTH levels. Imaging studies may also be done to locate any enlarged or abnormal parathyroid glands.

Other Options:
Option A: Hyperthyroidism is not associated with the occurrence of recurrent renal calculi; it presents with heat intolerance, weight loss, tremors, anxiety and ophthalmic symptoms. This patient doesn't have signs of hyperthyroidism.
Option C: Pinealoma is not associated with the occurrence of recurrent renal calculi; it presents with headaches, difficulty with eye movements, balance disturbances and difficulty walking. This patient doesn't have signs of pinealoma.
Option D: Bladder carcinoma is not associated with the occurrence of recurrent renal calculi; it presents hematuria, weight loss, loss of appetite, increased frequency, urgency and hesitancy. This patient doesn't have signs of bladder carcinoma.

stem: Patient came with a difficulty in extending the distal interphalangeal joint of medial 2 fingers. Injury to which of the following structures will result in inability to hold the paper between the fingers?
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Injury to the medial epicondyle of the humerus can affect the function of the ulnar nerve, which innervates the muscles responsible for finger and hand movements.
• Difficulty extending the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint of the medial two fingers (ring and little fingers) is a characteristic finding in such injuries.
• The specific muscles involved in extending the DIP joints are the extensor digitorum communis (EDC) and the extensor digiti minimi (EDM) muscles.
• The ulnar nerve innervates both and when injured or compressed, as a result of injury to the medial epicondyle, it can result in weakness or paralysis of the muscles it innervates.

Other Options:
Option A: Injury to the surgical neck of the humerus doesn't directly affect the function of the ulnar nerve, hence, it wouldn't cause the given symptoms. Instead, it affects the axillary nerve and results in weakness or paralysis of the deltoid muscle.
Option B: Injury to the lateral epicondyle of the humerus doesn't directly affect the function of the ulnar nerve, hence, it wouldn't cause the given symptoms. Instead, it can be associated with lateral epicondylitis or "tennis elbow", causing pain and weakness in the wrist or extensor muscles.
Option D: Injury to the spinal groove of the humerus doesn't directly affect the function of the ulnar nerve, hence, it wouldn't cause the given symptoms. Instead, it can be associated with radial nerve injury and cause weakness or paralysis of the muscles of the forearm, wrist and fingers that are innervated by the radial nerve.

stem: A patient presented to the emergency department with Pelvic instability after an RTA, what will be the first step in management?
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• When a patient presents with pelvic instability after a road traffic accident, the first crucial step would be immediate stabilisation of the pelvic region to prevent further injury and reduce the risk of life-threatening complications.
• Hence, bed sheet or pelvic binder draping is often used as an initial treatment measure in such cases. It provides external compression and stabilisation to the pelvic region, holds fractured bones together and maintains proper alignment, reducing the risk of further displacement or movement.
• Additionally, the pressure from the sheet provides support and can alleviate some of the pain associated with movement and helps control bleeding by reducing the space available for blood to accumulate and promotes clot formation. It also restricts movement and stabilises the pelvis, minimising the risk of additional trauma.
• Once the patient is stabilised, further management such as pelvic imaging, interventions or surgical repair may be necessary.

Other Options:
Option A: After initial stabilisation, imaging and further evaluation needs to be done to understand the extent and nature of the injury sustained, before proceeding with definite internal fixation.
Option B: After initial stabilisation, imaging and further evaluation needs to be done, before proceeding with external fixation.
Option C: After initial stabilisation, routine and special blood tests and imaging studies need to be done to make sure there is no internal bleeding. If there is, then group and crossmatch would be done before giving the patient a blood transfusion due to blood loss.

stem: Tibial Fracture, after 12 hours he became unconscious. Axillary petechial lesion, breathlessness, diagnosis?
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Fat embolism syndrome (FES) is a rare but serious condition that may occur after long bone fractures, such as a tibial fracture. This occurs due to fat globules originating from the bone marrow or adipose tissue that induce a toxic vasculitis followed by platelet-fibrin thrombosis and actual obstruction of small pulmonary vessels by fat molecules.
• It typically presents within the first 24 to 72 hours after the initial injury. The patient may become drowsy, restless, disoriented or unconscious and have tachypnoea, tachycardia and dyspnoea. These features are seen in this patient as well.
• Additionally, the axillary petechial rash is a classic finding in FES due to microhemorrhages caused by the rupture of blood vessels due to fat emboli.
• FES is a medical emergency and can be diagnosed clinically. Treatment involves respiratory support, heparinisation, IV LMW dextran and corticosteroids.

Other Options:
Option B: Compartment syndrome is characterised by increased pressure within a closed anatomical compartment, leading to compromised blood flow and tissue damage. It occurs commonly in the limbs after traumatic injuries or fractures. Symptoms are severe pain, swelling, numbness and loss of function.
Option C: Hypovolemic shock may be seen following fractures of long bones, such as tibial fractures. However, it is caused due to massive haemorrhage and wouldn't have symptoms like breathlessness. Additionally, there would be an identifiable source of bleeding.
Option D: Haemorrhagic shock may be seen following fractures of long bones, such as tibial fractures. However, it is caused due to a massive internal or external bleed and this would be identifiable.

stem: Which of the following ligaments is injured in the image shown below?
subject: Orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Acromioclavicular (AC) dislocation is an uncommon injury, caused due to a fall on the outer prominence of the shoulder. It leads to a partial displacement or separation of the acromioclavicular joint (as can be seen in the X-ray given), located between the acromion (part of the scapula) and the clavicle.
• This may be classified as Grade III as the lateral end of the clavicle is unusually prominent.
• Additionally, the X-ray shows visible displacement and joint space widening between the acromion and clavicle.
• Management may be conservative using triangular slings and analgesics or surgical repair (most likely as it is Grade III).

Other Options:
Option A: Glenohumeral injury would involve dislocation or subluxation of the humeral head from its normal position. This is not appreciated in the given X-ray, making it unlikely to be a glenohumeral injury.
Option B: Coracoacromial injury would involve damage to the coracoacromial arch. X-ray would show changes in the shape or structure of the acromion or coracoid process, such as bony spurs, calcifications and narrowing of the subacromial space. These features cannot be appreciated in the given X-ray, making it an unlikely diagnosis.
Option C: Gleno-acromial injury would show soft tissue swelling and increased density in the area of the ligament on X-ray. Additionally, the acromion may appear irregular or blunted and signs of impingement. These features cannot be appreciated in the given X-ray, making it an unlikely diagnosis.

stem: NADAS Criteria are used for:
options: Assessment of children of heart disease
Assessment of children of mental retardation
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Nada's criteria is a diagnostic criteria for congenital heart disease.
• It includes the following:
• Major criteria:
1. Systolic murmur of grade 3 or more.
2. Any diastolic murmur
3. Congestive heart failure (CHF)
4. Cyanosis
• Minor criteria:
1. Systolic murmur of grade 1 or 2
2. Abnormal second heart sound
3. Abnormal ECG findings
4. Abnormal chest X ray
5. Abnormal BP
• 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor criteria are essential for diagnosis.
MAJOR CRITERIAMINOR CRITERIA
SYSTOLIC MURMUR Grade 3 OR MORESYS. MURMUR LESS THAN Grade 3
DIASTOLIC MURMURABN. SECOND SOUND
CYANOSISABN. ECG
CHFABN. CXR
ABN. BP


Other Options:
Option A: Nada's criteria is not used for assessment of children with dehydration. IMNCI charts are available for the same which can be used easily by peripheral health care workers.
Option C: Nada's criteria is not used for assessment of children with malnutrition. Anthropometric measurements and growth charts can be used for the same.
Option D: Nada's criteria is not used for assessment of children with mental retardation. Developmental milestones and intelligence quotient can be used to assess the same.

stem: A 4 year old boy presents with redness in eyes, swollen red tongue with an enlarged lymph node in the neck as shown in the images below. What is the management of this condition?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The images show the following:
1. Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis
2. Red inflamed strawberry tongue, cracked lips
3. Red swollen hands (palms)
4. Red swollen feet, pedal edema
5. Maculopapular rash
6. Diaper rash
• All these are features of Kawasaki disease or mucocutaneous lymphadenopathy.
• It is a medium vessel vasculitis.
• It is the most common vasculitis seen in children.
• It commonly affects the coronary arteries.
• The most important drug of choice for Kawasaki disease is intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG).
• It is given at the dose of 2g/kg slow infusion.
• It is given along with aspirin 50 mg/kg/day till fever resolves.
• After that, aspirin is continued at a low antiplatelet dose of 5mg/kg/day.
• By treating Kawasaki disease, the incidence of coronary artery aneurysms and myocardial infarction significantly decrease.

Other Options:
Option B: IVIG is given along with aspirin 50 mg/kg/day till fever resolves. After that, aspirin is continued at a low antiplatelet dose of 5mg/kg/day. However, aspirin is not the first drug that is given in Kawasaki disease.
Option C: LMWH is not given in Kawasaki disease. It is a parenteral drug and is preferred in situations where immediate action is necessary or where warfarin in contraindicated (ex: pregnancy).
Option D: Blood transfusion is not given in Kawasaki disease. It does not cause decrease in blood corpuscles or bleeding manifestations.

stem: What is the complication caused by the organism associated with the given image?
subject: Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows a slapped cheek appearance.
• It is seen in the fifth disease.
• The causative organism is parvovirus B-19.
• It is a single stranded DNA virus.
• Its complications include:
1. Pure red cell aplasia
2. Aplastic anaemia in patients with other hemoglobinopathies
3. Chronic arthropathy
4. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
5. Hydrops fetalis
• Hence, the answer to this question is pure red cell aplasia.

Other Options:
Option B: Kaposi sarcoma is not caused by parvovirus B-19. It is caused by HHV-8 in patients infected with HIV. It is an AIDS defining malignancy.
Option C: Stomatitis is not caused by parvovirus B-19. It can be caused by a variety of causes like vitamin B complex deficiencies, iron deficiency etc.
Option D: Myocardial infarction is not caused by parvovirus B-19. Parvovirus rarely affects the heart and hence does not increase the risks of myocardial infarction.

stem: DPT vaccine is given to a child, 6 weeks following which the child develops inconsolable crying. In 10 weeks, what would be the next step?
options: Give DPT but after one month
subject: Pediatrics, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Inconsolable cry is a typical side effect of pertussis component of DPT vaccine.
• According to the national immunisation schedule, DPT is given as a part of the pentavalent vaccine at 6, 10, 14 weeks.
• Side effects of pertussis component are:
1. Inconsolable cry lasting for more than 3 hours within 48 hours of injection.
2. Hyperpyrexia within 48 hours of injection.
3. Hypotensive hyporesponsive syndrome within 48 hours of injection.
4. Seizures within 72 hours of injection.
• However, these are not contraindications of DPT and hence children can receive DPT at the next visit. Only precautions need to be taken in such situations.
• Contraindications of DPT vaccine include:
1. Progressive neurological disease.
2. Encephalopathy within 7 days of the previous vaccine.
3. Anaphylaxis after the previous vaccine.

Other Options:
Option B: Only DT is not the right answer as inconsolable cry is not the contraindication of pertussis vaccine.
Option C: Contraindications of DPT vaccine include progressive neurological disease, encephalopathy within 7 days of the previous vaccine and anaphylaxis after the previous vaccine. Hence, DPT can be given.
Option D: There is no indication for delaying the vaccination by one month. The contraindications of DPT vaccine are explained above.

stem:
A child presents to the clinic with 3 days of vomiting, watery diarrhoea and an altered sensorium. Possible diagnosis includes
1. Severe dehydration
2. Hyponatremia
3. Cerebral Venous Thrombosis
4. HUS
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Differential diagnosis for the cause of altered sensorium include:
1. Hypoglycemia
2. Hyponatremia
3. Hypernatremia
4. Cerebral venous thrombosis
5. Sagittal venous thrombosis
6. Severe dehydration
• Dysentery is a condition with blood in stools. It is commonly caused by shigella.
• Shigella can also cause toxin mediated encephalopathy and present with altered sensorium.
• Hemolytic uremic syndrome is caused by shigella.
• It should be suspected whenever a patient develops symptoms of renal failure or thrombocytopenia after an episode of dysentery.
• However, it follows dysentery and not diarrhoea.

Other Options:
Option A: This is not the correct answer as cerebral venous thrombosis can also lead to altered sensorium and it is not included in the option.
Options C and D: Hemolytic uremic syndrome occurs after dysentery, not diarrhoea. Hence, it cannot be included in the correct options.

stem:
What is the delivery procedure to be done in a newborn delivery through meconium stained liquor?
1. Before delivery of shoulder, suction of mouth and nose
2. PPV in non vigorous child after initial steps
3. Gentle suctioning of vigorous baby
4. Tracheal suctioning f/b mouth suctioning
subject: Pediatrics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• In recent times, the guidelines of managing a baby with meconium stained liquor has undergone changes. Nowadays, a baby born with meconium stained liquor is managed according to the normal neonatal resuscitation schedule.
• Positive pressure ventilation (PPV) with bag and mask is given at the end of one minute, if:
1. Heart rate is less than 100
2. Baby is apnoeic
• Gentle suctioning of a vigorous baby may be done, if required.
• Tracheal suctioning after intubation used to be done previously. But it was found that it has no added benefits.
• Intrapartum suctioning used to be done previously. But it was found that it has no added benefits.

Other Options:
Options A and B: Intrapartum suctioning used to be done previously. But it was found that it has no added benefits. Hence it is no longer done.
Option D: Tracheal suctioning after intubation used to be done previously. But it was found that it has no added benefits. Thus, this is not the correct answer.

stem: Which of the following syndromes is best associated with congenital heart disease?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution

• Holt Oram syndrome presents with the following features:
1. Congenital heart disease
2. Skeletal malformations

• Skeletal malformations include:
1. Thumb aplasia or hypoplasia
2. Radial bone hypoplasia
3. Clavicle hypoplasia

• Congenital heart conditions include:
1. Atrial septal defects (ASD)
2. First degree heart blocks
• It is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion.

Other Options:
Option A: Lesch Nyhan Syndrome (LNS) is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HPRT). LNS is an X-linked recessive disease carried by the female parent and passed on to a male child.
Option B: Rasmussen syndrome is a very rare condition that involves long-term worsening encephalitis of one hemisphere (half) of the brain. This inflammation leads to frequent seizures and progressive and permanent brain damage.
Option D: Leopard syndrome refers to Noonan syndrome with multiple lentigines (NSML), which presents with disorders of the skin, head and face, inner ear and heart.

stem: Which of the following is not a cause of high output cardiac failure?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• High output cardiac failure mainly has two causes:
1. Vasodilation
2. Shunts
• Shunt mechanism is seen in the following situations:
1. Arterio-venous fistulas
2. Cirrhosis with intrahepatic shunts
• Vasodilation mechanism is seen in the following situations:
1. Severe anaemia
2. Severe hypercapnia
3. Beri-beri or chronic thiamine deficiency
• Cor pulmonale refers to right heart failure following pulmonary artery hypertension and it does not lead to high output cardiac failure.

Other Options:
Option B: Beri-beri can cause high output cardiac failure. This occurs by systemic vasodilation as a result of a deficiency of thiamine in the body.
Option C: Anaemia can cause high output cardiac failure. This occurs by systemic vasodilation in response to the chronic hypoxic conditions present in the body.
Option D: Systemic arterio-venous shunt can cause high output cardiac failure. This occurs due to the shunt mechanism, which increases the workload on the heart.

stem: Where is the SRY gene located?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The SRY (Sex-determining Region Y) gene is located on the short arm of the Y chromosome, specifically at the region Yp11.3.
• This gene is responsible for initiating the development of male sex characteristics during embryogenesis.
• Mutations or abnormalities in the SRY gene can lead to disorders of sex development, wherein individuals may have atypical sexual development or ambiguous genitalia.

Other Options:
Option A: The SRY is not located on the long arm of the Y chromosome. However, the long arm of the Y chromosome does have important genes and genetic regions such as the AZF region, the TSPY gene and the RBMY gene.
Option C: The SRY gene is not located on the long arm of the X chromosome. However, the long arm of the X chromosome does have important genes and genetic regions such as the FMR1 gene, the DMD gene, the G6PD gene and the IDS gene.
Option D: The SRY gene is not located on the short arm of the X chromosome. However, the short arm of the X chromosome does have important genes and genetic regions such as the STS gene, the Xp21 locus region and the SHOX gene.

stem: In cases of complete gonadal dysgenesis, what type of mutation will be seen in the SRY Gene?
subject: Pathology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Complete gonadal dysgenesis, also known as Swyer syndrome, is a condition characterised by the failure of the gonads (ovaries or testes) to develop properly.
• In individuals who have this condition, there is a discrepancy between the chromosomal sex (typically, 46, XY) and the phenotypic sex (typically female).
• There are point mutations that can affect the SRY gene, which is responsible for initiating the development of testes during embryogenesis.

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: Complete gonadal dysgenesis is not typically associated with deletion or inversion or translocation of the SRY gene.

stem: A patient presents to the clinic with clitoromegaly, prepubertal breasts and Tanner 2 Pubic Hair and a blind ending vagina. The karyotype is 46 XY and testosterone levels are in the range of a male. Which of the following is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Complete Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS) is a genetic condition where individuals with XY chromosomes have a complete insensitivity to androgens due to deficiency of androgen receptors.
• The gonads are testes (undescended) and even though androgens are secreted, they are unable to act due to deficiency of androgen receptors.
• As a result during fetal life, the wolffian duct does not develop into male internal genitalia and the external genitalia fails to masculinize. AMH is secreted from the sertoli cells of the testes and mullerian ducts regress.
• As a result, these individuals are born and brought up as females, with uterus, vagina and tubes absent and only a blind vaginal pouch (the part that develops from the urogenital sinus) and external genitalia female.
• They are identified at puberty with primary amenorrhea, absent pubic hair, absent uterus, breasts are well developed due to peripheral aromatization of testosterone to estrogen.
• They have serum testosterone levels in the male range and the diagnosis is confirmed with karyotyping.

Other Options:
Option A: 5-alpha reductase deficiency is an autosomal recessive genetic condition that affects the conversion of testosterone into dihydrotestosterone (DHT). DHT is an important hormone involved in the development of male external genitalia during embryogenesis. This deficiency leads to impaired masculinisation of the external genitalia in individuals, such as this patient, with XY chromosomes. Affected individuals have ambiguous or female-appearing genitalia at birth. However, during puberty, there is an increase in testosterone levels which can lead to spontaneous virilisation.
Option B: Swyer syndrome (46, XY) or complete gonadal dysgenesis is a genetic disorder wherein individuals with XY chromosomes develop female external genitalia. The dysgenetic gonads (undescended) fail to secrete androgens and thus the male internal genitalia from the wolffian duct does not develop and the external genitalia remains female. The dysgenetic gonads fail to secrete AMH and thus the mullerian ducts do not regress but develop to give rise to internal genitalia of females. They are born and brought up as females and are identified at puberty with primary amenorrhea, uterus is present, with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. There is deficiency of estrogen, breasts fail to develop and FSH levels are elevated. Testosterone production is similar to normal females ad pubic hair are present.
Option D: 17-alpha hydroxylase deficiency is a rare form of congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Individuals with this deficiency have early-onset hypertension due to cortisol deficiency, ambiguous genitalia in males, delayed or absent puberty in both sexes, mineralocorticoid excess and decreased sex steroid production. These symptoms don't align with the symptoms described.

stem: A 26 year old woman with a BMI of 40 presents to the clinic. She also has a history of infertility for the past 6 years. On physical examination hirsutism is noted. What is the best next step?
options: Weight Reduction + Metformin + Ovulation Induction with Urinary Gonadotropins
Weight Reduction + Metformin + Ovulation Induction with Clomiphene
Finasteride + Metformin and wait for spontaneous conception
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• A 26-year-old with a BMI of 40 and a 6-year history of infertility along with hirsutism (excessive hair growth) could point towards a diagnosis of polycystic ovarian syndrome or PCOS.
• PCOS is a common endocrine disorder that affects women of reproductive age.
• There are hormonal imbalances, particularly high levels of androgens, that disrupt the normal menstrual cycle and can lead to irregular or absent periods, difficulty in conceiving and development of hirsutism.
• Other symptoms include acne, weight gain, insulin resistance and acanthosis nigricans.
• Further hormonal investigations may be required, but the most important tool in diagnosing PCOS is ultrasound. It confirms enlarged ovaries, follicles in the ovaries and may also show endometrial hyperplasia.
• Once confirmed, management includes lifestyle modifications such as weight reduction, since her BMI is 40, Metformin which rectifies endocrine and metabolic functions and improves fertility and ovulation induction using clomiphene which is the first line of treatment for infertility due to PCOS.

Other Options:
Option A: Weight reduction and Metformin are appropriate. However, the use of urinary gonadotrophins is not appropriate as the first line of treatment with ovulation induction for PCOS is with clomiphene.
Option C: Finasteride is used primarily for the treatment of male pattern hair loss and BPH, it is not typically prescribed as a first-line treatment for PCOS in women. Additionally, waiting for spontaneous conception is not appropriate as the patient has been infertile for 6 years already.
Option D: Finasteride is used primarily for the treatment of male pattern hair loss and BPH, it is not typically prescribed as a first-line treatment for PCOS in women.

stem: Which is the most common cause of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding in pubertal girls?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• The most common cause of abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) in pubertal girls is anovulation, accounting for around 55.7% cases.
• Anovulation refers to the absence or irregularity of ovulation, which can disrupt the normal hormonal patterns and menstrual cycle.
• During puberty, girls undergo hormonal changes that lead to development of regular menstrual cycles. However, in some cases, the hormonal regulation of the menstrual cycle may not be fully established, resulting in anovulatory cycles.
• Without ovulation, the normal hormonal signals and feedback mechanisms that regulate the menstrual cycle may be disrupted, leading to irregular, prolonged or heavy bleeding. Intermenstrual bleeding and absence of typical menstrual symptoms.

Other Options:
Option A: Endometriosis can cause AUB due to presence of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus. However, it is not as common as anovulation leading to AUB, and is seen more commonly in late teens or early twenties.
Option C: Malignancies such as endometrial cancer, cervical cancer, ovarian cancer, uterine cancer and metastasis from other cancers can cause AUB. However, it is not seen as commonly as anovulation and is seen more frequently in different age groups rather than pubertal groups.
Option D: Bleeding disorders such as haemophilia, platelet function disorders, coagulation factor deficiencies among others can cause AUB. However, it is not seen as commonly as anovulation and is a more rare condition.

stem: A patient who is a known case of Fibroid Uterus presents with acute abdomen, fever and leukocytosis in the 24th week of gestation. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Red degeneration or hemorrhagic degeneration, is a specific type of degenerative chance that can occur in uterine fibroids during pregnancy.
• It typically occurs during the second or third trimester and is more common in larger fibroids and may be associated with rapid growth.
• It can cause sudden and severe lower abdominal pain which can be localised or generalised, contractions, vaginal bleeding and tenderness. Fever and leukocytosis on blood tests may also be seen.
• An ultrasound or MRI might reveal characteristic findings, an enlarged, hypoechoic or heterogeneous fibroid with areas of increased vascularity.
• Treatment is conservative, with analgesics, bed rest and application of heat.

Other Options:
Option A: Torsion of a fibroid occurs when it has a stalk or pedicle which twists upon itself, resulting in severe abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting, abnormal vaginal bleeding, pelvic pressure or discomfort. This patient doesn't have symptoms that make it likely to be torsion.
Option C: Infection would present with fever and leukocytosis, however, the patient is already a known case of fibroid and the complication of red degeneration is more likely to be the cause of her symptoms.
Option D: Preterm labour is unlikely as it wouldn't present with severe abdominal pain, fever and leukocytosis.

stem: A 27 year old female presents with painful bleeding during menses and pain while having intercourse. On physical examination there is adnexal tenderness and the uterus is retroverted. Which investigation would be done to confirm the diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• The symptoms and signs seen in this patient are suggestive of endometriosis.
• Endometriosis refers to the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterus.
• Patients may have dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia (painful sexual intercourse), adnexal tenderness (tenderness around ovaries and fallopian tubes during pelvic examination), retroverted uterus (tilted backwards instead of the usual forward position).
• Definitive diagnosis requires a diagnostic laparoscopy, which would allow direct visualisation and biopsy of the abnormal endometrial tissue.
• Treatment options include pain management with medications, hormone therapy to suppress oestrogen and surgical interventions to remove endometrial adhesions.

Other Options:
Option A: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) can be used as an imaging modality to detect and evaluate endometriosis, however, it is not a definitive test and laparoscopy would still be required to obtain a biopsy sample.
Option C: Laparotomy would involve a larger incision and could have more complications whereas a laparoscopy would be a minimally invasive procedure and achieve the same results, making it the more preferred option.
Option D: Hysterosalpingography (HSG) is a diagnostic procedure that can help evaluate endometriosis to a certain extent, however, it is not a definitive test for diagnosing endometriosis.

stem: Which of the following is the feature of primary dysmenorrhea?
options: Pain is present for the first 48 hours and then it subsides
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Primary dysmenorrhoea refers to painful menstrual cramps that occur in the absence of any underlying pelvic pathology.
• Menstrual pain can be severe cramping or throbbing type pain in the lower abdomen that starts a few hours before or at the onset of menstruation and lasts for around 48 hours.
• Pain can be mild to severe and may be associated with nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, headache, fatigue and dizziness.

Other Options:
Option B: Dyspareunia refers to painful sexual intercourse and is not a feature of primary dysmenorrhoea as it doesn't have any correlation to menstruation and is seen commonly in pelvic pathologies such as endometriosis.
Option C: Post-coital bleeding is a type of abnormal uterine bleeding that occurs after sexual intercourse and can be due to various reasons such as vaginal dryness, fibroids, malignancies and is not related to menstruation or primary dysmenorrhoea.
Option D: Non-cyclical pain refers to cramps or menstrual-type of pain that occurs in the absence of menstruation. It is not related to menstruation or primary dysmenorrhoea.

stem: All of the following are causes of non immune hydrops, EXCEPT?
subject: Pediatrics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• ABO incompatibility can cause hemolytic disease of the newborn in certain cases, but does not lead to non-immune hydrops fetalis.
• Hydrops fetalis refers to the accumulation of fluid in two or more foetal compartments.
• It is caused due to non-immune factors such as genetic abnormalities, cardiac or pulmonary malfunctions, foetal infections, foetal anaemia and other foetal or placental abnormalities.
• Immune hydrops refers to hydrops occurring because of fetal hemolysis in the setting of Rh incompatibility between the mother & fetus.
• ABO incompatibility can lead to neonatal jaundice, but is not causative of non-immune hydrops fetalis.

Other Options:
Option A: Parvovirus-B19 infection during pregnancy can cause non-immune hydrops by directly infecting and damaging the red blood cell precursors in the foetal bone marrow, leading to severe anaemia.
Option B: Severe forms of thalassemia, such as homozygous beta-thalassemia major, can cause profound anaemia in the foetus, leading to non-immune hydrops fetalis.
Option C: Certain cardiovascular conditions, such as foetal cardiac arrhythmias or structural abnormalities, can disrupt normal foetal circulating, leading to fluid accumulation in foetal compartments, causing non-immune hydrops fetalis.

stem: A female presented to the Gynaecology OPD with complaints of vaginal discharge. Upon further investigation, clue cells were found in microscopy. What are these clue cells?
options: Macrophage which have phagocytosed doderlein bacilli
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has a vaginal infection called bacterial vaginosis (BV).
• Vaginal discharge with clue cells is a characteristic finding BV.
• Clue cells are vaginal epithelial cells that are studded with bacteria, specifically Gardnerella vaginalis, and are visible under the microscope.
• The adherence of these bacteria to the surface of the vaginal cells gives them a stippled or "clue-like" appearance.
• The presence of these clue cells is a diagnostic criteria for BV.
• BV is characterised by an overgrowth of bacteria in the vaginal flora, leading to a disruption in the normal balance of bacteria.

Other Options:
Option A: While Gardnerella vaginalis is a rod-shaped bacterium, the accumulation of these rods are not known as clue cells.
Option C: Presence of macrophages with phagocytosed Doderlein bacteria is not typically seen in BV and is not associated with clue cells.
Option D: None of the above is incorrect as "epithelial cells studded with bacteria" is an accurate description of clue cells.

stem: A woman presents to the clinic with complaints of non-foul smelling white vaginal discharge. On further investigations, pH is found to be acidic and normal lactobacillus count is found. There is no pruritus and inflammation. Which is the probable cause?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Physiological vaginal discharge refers to the normal, healthy discharge of the vagina. It is natural and helps maintain the health of the vaginal environment.
• This discharge is generally clear or white in colour and has a mild or no odour, and its consistency may range from thin and watery to slightly thick and sticky.
• The amount of physiological discharge varies throughout the menstrual cycle. The pH ranges from 3.8-4.5, i.e., it is acidic and the normal vaginal flora has an abundance of lactobacilli.
• There is usually no discomfort, pruritis or inflammation, which if present, could be a sign of infection.
• This patient's vaginal discharge has all the features of a normal physiological vaginal discharge.

Other Options:
Option B: In candida infection, the discharge is typically thick, white and clumpy, resembling cottage cheese. It has a distinct odour, often described as yeasty or bread-like. It is also accompanied by itching, redness, and swelling of the vulva and vaginal area.
Option C: In chlamydia infection, the discharge is minimal and may appear as a clear or slightly milky discharge. It may not have a strong odour but causes burning micturition, pelvic pain and increased urinary frequency.
Option D: In bacterial vaginosis (BV), the discharge is typically thin, watery and greyish-white in colour. It has a distinct fishy odour and is usually accompanied by itching and irritation.

stem: Which of the following is incorrectly matched?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Colposuspension is a surgical procedure that involves placement of sutures or synthetic mesh to support the bladder neck and urethra, helping restore normal urinary continence.
• Cystocele refers to the descent or prolapse of the bladder into the vaginal wall.
• Colposuspension is primarily used to treat stress urinary incontinence and is not typically the procedure of choice to treat cystocele.
• Cystocele is usually managed through procedures specifically designed to repair the pelvic organ prolapse, such as anterior colporrhaphy or vaginal mesh placement.

Other Options:
Option B: Sacrospinous fixation is a surgical procedure used to treat vaginal vault prolapse, which occurs when the upper part of the vagina descends or protrudes into the vaginal canal or outside the body following a hysterectomy. It involved suturing of the sacrospinous ligament to the vaginal vault for support and suspension.
Option C: Vaginal hysterectomy is a surgical procedure used to treat uterine prolapse, which occurs when the uterus descends or protrudes into the vaginal canal or outside the body. The procedure involves separation of the uterus and its removal through the vaginal opening, while preserving the cervix in some cases.
Option D: Posterior perineal colporrhaphy is a surgical procedure used to repair a rectocele (where the rectum bulges into the posterior vaginal wall) to provide support to the rectum and restore the normal anatomy.

stem: A semen analysis shows absent fructose. Which of the following is the probable cause?
subject: Physiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Absent fructose in semen analysis can indicate a condition called congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD).
• CBAVD is a genetic disorder characterised by the absence or malformation of the vas deferens.
• In normal semen, fructose is produced by the seminal vesicles and contributes to the energy supply of sperm.
• However, in cases of absent seminal vesicles and obstruction of the ejaculatory ducts, the production and release of fructose into the semen are affected. These result in the absence of fructose in the semen analysis.

Other Options:
Option A: Absent seminal vesicles does affect the production and release of fructose into the semen. However, obstruction of the ejaculatory duct is also a factor that affects it.
Option B: Obstruction of the ejaculatory duct affects the production and release of fructose into the semen. However, absence of seminal vesicles also affects it.
Option D: Testicular carcinoma typically doesn't cause absent fructose in semen, which is more common with conditions affecting the seminal vesicles or ejaculatory ducts.

stem: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: At 8 week gestation, zygote is bipotential
DHT is necessary for development of external genitalia
MIS inhibits the formation of Mullerian Ducts
Wolffian Duct forms the male internal genitalia
subject: Embryology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Biopotential refers to the ability of an organism to develop either male or female reproductive structures.
• This state persists till about 5 to 6 weeks of gestation in all fetuses whether genetically male or female.
• By 6 weeks of gestation, the primordial germ cells reach the developing gonadal ridge from the yolk sac, where they are first identified.
• Once the germ cells reach the developing gonad, gonadal differentiation begins.
• In genetic males, the gonads develop into testes between 6 to 9 weeks of gestation.
• In genetic females, the gonads develop into ovaries between 8 to 10 weeks of gestation.
• Thus, at 8 week gestation the zygote is no longer bipotential.

Other Options:
Option B: Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) is a potent androgen and plays a crucial role in the development of male external genitalia. It binds to and activates androgen receptors in target tissues, promoting the development of the penis, scrotum and other structures.
Option C: Mullerian inhibiting substance (MIS) is a hormone secreted by the developing testes (Sertoli cells) in males. One of its primary functions is to inhibit the development of Mullerian ducts in male embryos, preventing the formation of female reproductive structures.
Option D: Wolffian ducts are responsible for the development of male internal genitalia in embryonic development. Testosterone promotes the development and differentiation of the Wolffian ducts into the epididymis, vas deferens, and seminal vesicles.

stem: Which of the following is NOT transmitted perinatally?
subject: Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Rubella is a viral infection caused by the rubella virus. It is generally a mild illness, but can have serious consequences if contracted during pregnancy.
• It can lead to congenital rubella syndrome, which can cause a range of birth defects and complications in the developing foetus, including hearing loss, cataracts, cardiac abnormalities, and development delays.
• Transmission can be prevented by immunisation with the MMR vaccine.
• It is transmitted from mother to foetus via the placenta (transplacental, intrauterine) and is not transmitted perinatally (intrapartum).

Other Options:
Option A: Cytomegalovirus infection can pass from the mother to the foetus transplacentally, during delivery (perinatal) or breastfeeding.
Option C: Herpes simplex virus infection can pass from the mother to the foetus intrauterine via the placenta and intrapartum (perinatally).
Option D: Hepatitis B infection can pass from the mother to the foetus perinatally.

stem: Which of the following investigations is NOT done for evaluation in Turner Syndrome?
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Antinuclear antibody (ANA) testing is not used to evaluate Turner syndrome as it is not specific for this condition.
• Turner syndrome is a genetic disorder caused by complete or partial absence of one of the X chromosomes (XO) in females.
• The diagnosis of Turner syndrome is primarily based on clinical features, such as short stature, delayed puberty and characteristic physical findings.
• Specific diagnostic tests including karyotype analysis, hormonal evaluations, cardiac evaluations, imaging studies, OGTT and audiometry may be used.
• ANA testing is used to evaluate autoimmune disorders such as SLE.
• Turner syndrome is not an autoimmune condition and ANA is not considered necessary or informative in its diagnosis.

Other Options:
Option A: Turner syndrome is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular abnormalities, such as coarctation of the aorta, bicuspid aortic valve and aortic dilation. Hence, cardiac evaluation using echocardiography and other imaging modalities helps assess these abnormalities.
Option B: Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) is employed to assess how the body processes glucose. Turner syndrome is associated with an increased risk of impaired glucose metabolism, including glucose intolerance and type 2 diabetes.
Option C: Hearing loss or hearing impairment is relatively common in individuals with Turner syndrome. Audiometry is performed to assess hearing function and detect any hearing loss that may require intervention or management.

stem: Which of the following is a true statement for HRT?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) has been shown to have potential benefits in relation to colon cancer.
• Some studies have suggested that the use of oestrogen-only HRT may be associated with a decreased risk of colon cancer.
• Oestrogen has been found to have potential protective effects on the colon, such as reducing inflammation and promoting cell differentiation and apoptosis.

Other Options:
Option A: HRT has been associated with an increased risk of breast cancer, especially when used long-term, over 5 years.
Option B: HRT has been associated with an increased risk of endometrial cancer, especially when oestrogen-only HRT is used. It stimulates the growth of the endometrium and in the absence of progestin, can lead to increased risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer.
Option D: HRT can help decrease the risk of osteoporosis in postmenopausal women. Oestrogen plays a crucial role in maintaining bone density, and during menopause, the decline in oestrogen can lead to bone loss and an increased risk of osteoporosis. HRT slows down the bone loss and prevents osteoporosis and fractures.

stem: Which of the following drugs is not used in the management of Endometriosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Endometriosis is characterised by the growth of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus.
• The primary goal of the treatment is to alleviate pain and infertility while slowing the progression of the disease.
• Oestrogen, particularly estradiol, is not used as a standalone treatment due to the potential for it to stimulate the growth and proliferation of the endometriotic implants, worsening the condition.
• Therefore, treatment approaches typically involve hormonal suppression or modulation to create an environment that limits the growth and activity of endometriotic tissue.

Other Options:
Option A: Leuprolide is a GnRH agonist. It is used as a short-term treatment or preoperatively to reduce the size of endometriotic implants.
Option C: Medroxyprogesterone acetate is a progestin hormone that works by suppressing the growth and activity of the endometrium, including endometriotic implants. It can alleviate the symptoms and slow the progression.
Option D: Clomiphene is primarily used in endometriosis to induce ovulation where there is coexisting ovulatory dysfunction or infertility.

stem:
Which of these will require evaluation for primary amenorrhea?
1. Absence of menses by age 15
2. No menses within 3 years of thelarche
3. High TSH with short stature
4. Absence of thelarche by the age of 13
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Menstruation usually begins by the age of 15 years, if not started by this age, it is considered primary amenorrhoea and needs to be evaluated.
• Thelarche refers to the development of breast tissue. If there are signs of breast development but have not experienced menstruation within three years of thelarche, further evaluation is needed.
• Elevated thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) levels may indicate an underlying thyroid disorder, such as hypothyroidism. This can affect reproductive function and contribute to amenorrhoea. Additionally, short stature can be associated with certain genetic conditions that can affect reproductive development.
• Thelarche typically occurs by 8-13 years. If there are no signs of breast development by age 13, it suggests a delay in puberty and needs further evaluation.
• Further evaluation refers to hormone level testing, imaging studies, genetic testing and identifying the cause of primary amenorrhoea.

Other Options:
Option A: Absence of menses by 15 years of age and no menses within 3 years of thelarche need evaluation; however, high TSH with short stature and absence of thelarche by age 13 are also factors that could need evaluation for primary amenorrhoea.
Option B: High TSH with short stature and absence of thelarche by age 13 need evaluation; however, absence of menses by 15 years of age and no menses within 3 years of thelarche need evaluation also need evaluation for primary amenorrhoea.
Option C: Absence of menses by 15 years of age and high TSH with short stature need evaluation; however, no menses within 3 years of thelarche and absence of thelarche by age 13 years also need evaluation for primary amenorrhoea.

stem: Which of the following is responsible for foetal heart rate acceleration?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Foetal heart rate (FHR) acceleration is a physiological response that indicates a healthy and responsive foetal autonomic nervous system.
• The autonomic function of the brainstem, specifically the medulla oblongata, plays a crucial role in regulating heart rate.
• During foetal movements, sensory input from the foetal body is transmitted to the brainstem, specifically the reticular formation, via different pathways.
• This sensory input triggers a reflex response mediated by the autonomic nervous system, resulting in FHR acceleration.

Other Options:
Option B: ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide) is a hormone secreted by the atria in response to increased blood volume and pressure. It primarily regulates fluid and electrolyte balance and has limited direct influence on FHR acceleration.
Option C: Carotid baroreceptors are specialised sensory receptors located in the carotid sinus. They monitor changes in blood pressure and help regulate it by transmitting signals to the brainstem to adjust the heart rate and vascular tone. Their influence on FHR acceleration is indirect and secondary to the autonomic response triggered by foetal movements.
Option D: Aortic baroreceptors are specialised sensory receptors located in the aortic arch. Their influence on FHR acceleration is also indirect and secondary to the autonomic response triggered by foetal movements.

stem: A 32 year old woman visits the OPD with h/o painless ulcer on vulva, her obstetric history reveals h/o still birth at 28 weeks. She has got contact with multiple sexual partners. What is the best next step?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This patient has a painless ulcer on her vulva, this is suggestive of syphilis.
• Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum.
• It can cause genital ulcers called chancres.
• Syphilis can be transmitted from mother to foetus during pregnancy, leading to adverse outcomes such as stillbirth or congenital syphilis, the former is seen in the patient.
• Additionally, multiple sexual partners increase the risk of acquiring STIs, including syphilis.
• The venereal disease laboratory test (VDRL test) is a screening test for syphilis, and if positive, further testing such as the Treponema pallidum particle agglutination test (TPPA) and fluorescent treponemal antibody absorption test (FTA-ABS) may be required.

Other Options:
Option B: HIV ELISA test is used to diagnose HIV infection or AIDS. This patient does not have symptoms commonly found in patients who may be HIV positive. However, since the patient has a history of multiple sexual partners, this test can be done once syphilis has been diagnosed and treated.
Option C: Lupus anticoagulant test is used to detect the presence of lupus anticoagulant antibodies that interfere with normal blood clotting, leading to an increased risk of abnormal blood clot formation. This test is employed in cases of recurrent miscarriages, which is not the case in this patient.
Option D: None of the above is not the correct answer as a VDRL test is needed in this patient to confirm the diagnosis of suspected syphilis.
